summary: Design + architect a modern cross-platform OpenCPN successor (macOS/iPad/iPhone) with on-demand satellite/Navionics charts, PredictWind routing, embedded Windy layers
logs: Per-surface design: macOS hero, on-demand chart + layers UX, iPad, iPhone

research.topic: ChartLocker-style on-demand chart acquisition for the "Helm" cross-platform marine nav app: tile pipeline, per-source ToS reality, Navionics path, and PredictWind/Windy overlays
research.summary: ChartLocker (chartlocker.brucebalan.com) is NOT software or an API — it is a hand-curated catalog of pre-built regional .mbtiles ZIPs (Bing/Google/ArcGIS satellite + rasterized Navionics) that cruisers download from MediaFire and load into OpenCPN 5.x using Chart Groups + quilting. The "magic" the user admires is entirely manual: Bruce Balan and Alene Rice download imagery for a region, georeference it, run it through GDAL/gdal2tiles into mbtiles, and post the ZIPs. Making it LIVE/"select-an-area, fetch-on-demand" is technically straightforward (bbox -> XYZ tile list -> download -> repackage as mbtiles -> cache offline) using off-the-shelf GDAL tooling. The hard problem is NOT engineering — it is legal/ToS. Three of the four imagery sources ChartLocker uses (Google, Bing, ArcGIS/Esri) explicitly PROHIBIT bulk download, scraping, and offline caching for redistribution in a commercial app; doing it live at scale in a shipping product is a contractual violation and a real takedown/litigation risk that a fundable company cannot ship. Navionics is the sharpest case: their Web API ToS explicitly forbids accessing tiles "through interfaces or channels (including undocumented Navionics interfaces) other than the Maps API(s)" and forbids reverse engineering — so ChartLocker's rasterized-Navionics tiles are a gray-to-prohibited cruiser hack you cannot legitimately ship. The clean, shippable path exists: Sentinel-2 (Copernicus, free, open license, commercial use OK with attribution) + NOAA ENC/RNC (US public domain) + OpenSeaMap (ODbL) for the on-demand pipeline, with the OFFICIAL Navionics/Garmin Web API or Mobile SDK (paid, approval-gated commercial license) bolted on for those who want Navionics legitimately. For weather: PredictWind has NO public API (only website map-embedding), and Windy is a Leaflet/WebView plugin (free GFS tier or ~$720/yr Pro) — both are embeddable in a WebView but neither gives you clean native overlay tiles or programmatic routing, so the "optimal route" overlay realistically requires the user's own PredictWind subscription + GPX export, not a server integration. Also note: there is no native iOS OpenCPN (App Store rules block it), so the iPad/iPhone targets mean a new touch UI, not a port.

findings.claim: ChartLocker is a curated catalog of pre-built regional mbtiles ZIPs, not software or an on-demand service — the 'select-an-area, fetch' behavior the user wants does not exist in ChartLocker today.
findings.detail: ChartLocker (Bruce Balan + Alene D. Rice) distributes pre-built mbtiles as ZIP files via MediaFire links: 'Click the link in the Area column to download a ZIP file containing the MBTile charts for that area.' Each region offers separate sets for Bing Satellite, Google Satellite, ArcGIS, and rasterized Navionics (and C-Map for a few areas). Coverage = Pacific Mexico, Central America, South Pacific, Bermuda, Caribbean/Bahamas. Files are large and per-source per-region: Fiji ranges 736 MB (Navionics) to 5.93 GB (BingSat). Typical zoom span is Z8-Z18 (minus Z17); older sets used Z12/Z15/Z18. Requires OpenCPN 5.x (mbtiles unsupported in 4.x). The georeferencing/tiling is done by hand by the authors before posting. So the 'live fetch-on-demand' version is a NEW build, not a feature of ChartLocker.

findings.claim: The 'rasterized Navionics' in ChartLocker is a screen-capture/scrape of the Navionics web tiles — a cruiser hack, NOT vector charts and NOT a licensed feed.
findings.detail: ChartLocker itself notes its Navionics mbtiles 'are no longer true vector charts; they are raster MBTiles at various zoom levels' — i.e. the vector Navionics web map was captured and re-tiled as raster images. This is exactly what Navionics' Web API ToS prohibits (see Navionics finding). It works for individual cruisers under the radar but is not a defensible basis for a shipping commercial product.

findings.claim: The on-demand tile pipeline (bbox -> tiles -> mbtiles -> offline cache) is technically simple and fully off-the-shelf with GDAL.
findings.detail: Pipeline: (1) user draws a bbox; (2) convert bbox + zoom range to an XYZ tile list (standard bbox_to_xyz); (3) fetch each tile from an XYZ/TMS source; (4) merge into a GeoTIFF or feed directly to mbtiles; (5) gdal2tiles.py generates the tile pyramid (supports XYZ or TMS, zoom ranges like '8-18', --processes N for parallelism), or GDAL's MBTiles driver writes the sqlite mbtiles directly; (6) OpenCPN reads the mbtiles via quilting + Chart Groups. Critical coordinate gotcha: mbtiles uses TMS Y-axis convention (y=0 at south), inverted from XYZ slippy-map (y=0 at north) — must flip Y on ingest. For already-georeferenced sources (Sentinel-2 GeoTIFF, NOAA) no manual georeferencing is needed; only ad-hoc screenshots need ground-control-point warping (gdalwarp). Storage math below sets the scale.

findings.claim: Tile/storage math: ground resolution and tile counts scale 4x per zoom, so high-zoom satellite over a cruising area is GB-scale — budget accordingly.
findings.detail: Web-Mercator tile side length = 40,075,017 m / 2^z: Z16 ~611 m/tile, Z17 ~305 m/tile, Z18 ~153 m/tile (256px tiles). Each zoom adds ~4x the tiles of the previous. A single 256x256 tile averages ~15 KB but satellite PNGs run larger (often 30-100+ KB). Rule of thumb consistent with ChartLocker's own sizes: a moderate cruising region (a few hundred km of coastline) at Z8-Z18 = hundreds of MB to several GB per imagery source (Fiji BingSat = 5.93 GB). Implication for on-demand: cap default max zoom (e.g. Z16-17), let the user opt into Z18 for anchorages, and stream/cache incrementally rather than fetching whole regions at once.

findings.claim: Google Maps/Earth imagery: bulk tile download and offline caching for your own app is explicitly PROHIBITED — cannot ship.
findings.detail: Google Maps Platform ToS: customers 'will not export, extract, or otherwise scrape Google Maps Content for use outside the Services, and specifically will not bulk download Google Maps tiles.' Caching is barred except narrow ID/value exceptions (place_id, lat/long for <=30 days) — none of which cover imagery tiles. Note the legal nuance: US courts have at times found scraping *public* data not per se illegal (hiQ-style), but that does NOT cure the contractual ToS breach, and Google actively enforces. Verdict: PROHIBITED for a shipping product. Google Earth's separately-licensed imagery is also not redistributable.

findings.claim: Bing Maps imagery: not supported for offline use; Bing Maps for Enterprise is being sunset (ends 2028) — do not build on it.
findings.detail: Bing Maps is 'not supported for offline use,' and even within Esri's ArcGIS Pro the 'keep cache between sessions' option is disabled specifically for Bing. Microsoft is winding Bing Maps Enterprise down: no new licensees after June 30 2024, full end-of-support June 30 2028, with migration pushed to Azure Maps. So Bing is both contractually unsuitable for offline caching and a dying platform. Verdict: PROHIBITED / do-not-build.

findings.claim: Esri/ArcGIS World Imagery: the default basemap forbids tile export; only the licensed 'World Imagery (for Export)' layer permits offline caching, capped and under Esri's Master License — a paid, constrained path, not free redistribution.
findings.detail: The standard World Imagery layer 'is not intended to be used to export tiles for offline use.' Esri provides a separate 'World Imagery (for Export)' service for ArcGIS apps, default-capped at 100,000 tiles per export and governed by the Esri Master License Agreement. Esri's general terms bar reproduction/derivative works for commercial purposes 'except as expressly permitted in writing.' Verdict: GRAY/licensable — possible only via a paid ArcGIS subscription within Esri's own SDK/app ecosystem and within the tile cap; not a free, ship-anywhere source. Underlying imagery is also third-party (Maxar etc.) with its own constraints.

findings.claim: Sentinel-2 (ESA Copernicus) is the clean, SHIPPABLE imagery source: free, open license, commercial use allowed, redistribution/derivatives permitted with attribution.
findings.detail: Copernicus Sentinel data is 'free, full and open' under EU regulation: users may reproduce, distribute, communicate to the public, adapt, modify, and combine the data — including commercial use — at no cost. Only requirement: attribution notice 'Copernicus Sentinel data [Year]' (and 'Contains modified Copernicus Sentinel data [Year]' if altered). Available as analysis-ready GeoTIFFs (already georeferenced) via the Copernicus Data Space Ecosystem, so it drops straight into the gdal2tiles pipeline. THE catch: 10 m native resolution. Great for broad context, reef/shoal detection in clear shallow water (the blue band aids shallow-water imaging), and satellite-derived bathymetry valid to ~10 m depth at ~IHO Zone-of-Confidence C (~1.9 m accuracy) — but NOT sub-meter, so it is coarser than the Bing/Google imagery ChartLocker users prize for close-in anchorage navigation.

findings.claim: NOAA ENC/RNC and OpenSeaMap are additional safe, shippable sources — US charts are public domain; OpenSeaMap is open-licensed.
findings.detail: NOAA raster (RNC, BSB) and vector (ENC, S-57) charts are US Government works, free and effectively public domain, downloadable in bulk; NOAA even publishes its own rescheme ENC MBTiles (distribution.charts.noaa.gov/ncds) usable directly in OpenCPN. Caveat: NOAA retired its RNC *tile* service (Oct 2021) and seamless RNC service (Jan 2022), and is sunsetting traditional raster paper charts in favor of ENC — so prefer ENC/NCDS mbtiles. OpenSeaMap is community nautical data under ODbL (open, attribution + share-alike), available as mbtiles, but is unofficial and lacks complete soundings — an overlay, not a primary chart. Together with Sentinel-2 these form a 100%-legal default chart stack.

findings.claim: Navionics: there is NO open public tile API; scraping the web tiles is explicitly forbidden by ToS; the only legitimate path is the approval-gated, paid Garmin/Navionics Web API or Mobile SDK.
findings.detail: Navionics Web API ToS: 'you must not access map tiles or imagery through interfaces or channels (including undocumented Navionics interfaces) other than the Maps API(s)' and 'must not reverse engineer, decompile or otherwise attempt to extract the source code.' That squarely prohibits the ChartLocker-style rasterization. The legitimate route is via Garmin Developers (developer.garmin.com/marine-charts): a Web API (Standard = free in-window viewer, no overlays; Enhanced = paid annual commercial license, regional/global, allows custom overlays + full-page) and a separate Navionics Mobile SDK — both require a developer request and approval. Crucially, these are designed for ONLINE display within their SDK; they are not a license to bulk-cache Navionics tiles into your own offline mbtiles. So 'offline Navionics, on-demand' is essentially not offered even on the paid path — the user must accept either (a) official online Navionics via SDK, or (b) no Navionics and rely on the open stack.

findings.claim: PredictWind has NO public API — weather routing cannot be integrated server-side. Only website map-embedding is offered; the realistic overlay path is user-driven GPX export.
findings.detail: PredictWind FAQ: 'PredictWind does not offer a public API. Our current business model is focused on providing a complete business-to-consumer platform.' No programmatic access to the optimal route or departure-planning output. Their only third-party offering is embedding their weather maps on a website. Their router runs server-side (output route file is tiny, 15-25 KB). Practical integration for Helm: let the user run routing in PredictWind's own app/site, EXPORT the optimal route as GPX, and IMPORT that GPX as a route layer in Helm (OpenCPN already imports GPX). A true live 'optimal route overlay' computed inside Helm is not possible against PredictWind without a partnership that does not currently exist.

findings.claim: Windy weather layers ARE embeddable, but as a Leaflet/JavaScript WebView widget — not native overlay tiles you can composite onto the OpenCPN canvas, and the free tier is GFS-only.
findings.detail: Windy's Map Forecast API is a Leaflet 1.4.x JS plugin ('for web pages and mobile devices,' requires leaflet.js + libBoot.js) exposing wind/gust/rain/clouds/temperature/pressure/waves/currents (40-51 layers). Free 'Testing' tier is dev-only and not allowed in production and is limited to the GFS model + core layers; the Professional tier (~$720/yr, paid annually) unlocks iconEu/NAM, most layers, no ads, and production use. It does NOT expose raw weather tiles for native compositing — integration means hosting the Windy Leaflet map in a WebView. So in Helm you'd either (a) show Windy in a dedicated WebView weather panel, or (b) for true native overlay on the chart, pull GRIB (e.g. from a GRIB source / the existing OpenCPN GRIB plugin) and render wind/waves/currents yourself rather than via Windy's widget.

findings.claim: There is NO native iOS OpenCPN (App Store rules block it) and the iPad/iPhone targets therefore require a brand-new touch UI, not a port — though macOS does have a native universal (Intel+Apple Silicon) build.
findings.detail: OpenCPN FAQ: 'There is no iPhone version of OpenCPN... The iPhone store restricts production of OpenCPN' (GPL/App-Store-policy conflict); the suggested workaround is VNC to a Raspberry Pi. Android has an official Play Store build. macOS official packages are universal binaries (native on both architectures). Consequence for Helm: macOS can reuse the OpenCPN core/engine; iPad/iPhone must be a new SwiftUI/touch front-end (and likely a clean-room or relicensed core, given GPL + App Store interaction), reusing chart/mbtiles formats and the navigation logic rather than the wxWidgets UI.

constraints: PROHIBITED to ship: bulk-downloading/offline-caching Google Maps/Earth tiles (ToS bars bulk tile download and scraping for use outside their Services).
PROHIBITED / do-not-build: Bing Maps imagery for offline caching (not supported for offline use) AND Bing Maps Enterprise is end-of-life June 30 2028, no new licensees since June 2024.

GRAY / licensable only: Esri/ArcGIS World Imagery offline export requires the special 'World Imagery (for Export)' layer, a paid ArcGIS subscription, the Esri Master License, and a default 100,000-tile export cap — usable only inside Esri's own SDK/apps, not free redistribution.

PROHIBITED: scraping/rasterizing Navionics web tiles (the ChartLocker method) — Navionics ToS forbids accessing tiles via any interface other than the official Maps API and forbids reverse engineering. The ONLY legitimate Navionics path is the approval-gated, paid Garmin/Navionics Web API (Enhanced) or Mobile SDK, and even that is designed for ONLINE display, not offline mbtiles caching.

Sentinel-2/Copernicus is SAFE for commercial use and redistribution but ONLY with attribution ('Copernicus Sentinel data [Year]' / 'Contains modified Copernicus Sentinel data [Year]') — and its 10 m resolution is coarser than the sub-meter Bing/Google imagery cruisers value for close-in reef/anchorage work.
NOAA ENC/RNC are US public domain and safe; OpenSeaMap is open (ODbL, attribution + share-alike) but unofficial and lacks complete soundings — overlay only, not primary chart.
PredictWind has NO public API — no server-side routing integration is possible; the only viable path is user-driven export of the optimal route as GPX and import into Helm.

Windy is embeddable only as a Leaflet/JS WebView widget (not native composite tiles); free tier is GFS-only and dev-only/non-production; production/commercial use needs the ~$720/yr Professional tier.

No native iOS OpenCPN exists (App Store policy blocks it); iPad/iPhone must be a new touch UI, and GPL-vs-App-Store interaction means the mobile core likely needs relicensing or clean-room reimplementation.

Satellite imagery for navigation is supplemental, not authoritative: clouds hide reefs, imagery can have 'painted out' reefs, and satellite-derived bathymetry is only ~IHO ZOC-C (~1.9 m) to ~10 m depth — a prominent 'not for primary navigation, cross-reference official charts' disclaimer is mandatory.
research.recommendation:
Ship the on-demand pipeline on a LEGAL imagery stack and treat the prohibited sources as user-supplied-only. Concretely:

1) ON-DEMAND ENGINE (build this): user draws a bbox + picks a max zoom -> generate the XYZ tile list -> fetch -> write to mbtiles via GDAL (gdal2tiles / MBTiles driver), remembering the TMS Y-flip -> cache offline -> expose to the chart engine via OpenCPN-style quilting + Chart Groups. This is the ChartLocker 'magic' made live, and it is the same engine regardless of source.

2) DEFAULT (shippable, 100% legal) SOURCES wired into that engine: Sentinel-2 (Copernicus, free/open/commercial-OK, georeferenced GeoTIFFs, attribute it) for satellite imagery; NOAA ENC/RNC + NOAA's own NCDS mbtiles for US official charts; OpenSeaMap (ODbL) as a seamarks overlay. This trio lets Helm fetch-on-demand for any bbox worldwide with zero legal exposure. Be upfront that Sentinel-2 is 10 m, not sub-meter — pair it with satellite-derived-bathymetry shading where it helps and a clear accuracy disclaimer.

3) NAVIONICS / GOOGLE / BING / ESRI (do NOT scrape): do not build a server that fetches these. Instead (a) integrate the OFFICIAL Garmin/Navionics Web API or Mobile SDK as an in-app ONLINE chart layer for users who want Navionics and accept it is online-only (apply via Garmin Developers, paid Enhanced license); and (b) keep ChartLocker compatibility by letting users IMPORT their own .mbtiles files (BYO tiles) — this is exactly how OpenCPN works today and keeps you out of the redistribution chain entirely. That single 'import mbtiles' feature preserves everything ChartLocker users already do without your company touching a prohibited tile.

4) PREDICTWIND: no API exists, so do not promise a live in-app optimal-route engine against PredictWind. Implement GPX import of the user's PredictWind-exported optimal route as a route layer (OpenCPN already does GPX). If you want native routing later, license a routing engine you control or pursue a formal PredictWind partnership — but scope the v1 around GPX import.

5) WINDY: embed Windy's Leaflet widget in a dedicated WebView weather panel (Professional tier, ~$720/yr, for production), and SEPARATELY implement true native overlay of wind/gust/rain/waves/currents on the chart via GRIB rendering (reuse the OpenCPN GRIB approach) so the on-chart weather is yours and offline-capable, with Windy as the rich online view.

6) PLATFORMS: reuse the OpenCPN core on the native macOS universal build; build iPad/iPhone as a new SwiftUI touch front-end over a relicensed/clean-room nav core (App Store blocks shipping OpenCPN itself). Keep mbtiles/GPX/ENC as the shared data formats across all three.

Bottom line: the engineering is the easy 20%; the legal source-selection is the load-bearing 80%. A fundable product ships the on-demand engine on Sentinel-2 + NOAA + OpenSeaMap, supports user-imported mbtiles (the ChartLocker bridge), and integrates Navionics/Windy/PredictWind only through their official/legal surfaces — never by scraping.

research.topic: Integrating Windy weather layers (and PredictWind routing + on-demand chart acquisition) into the "Helm" cross-platform OpenCPN-derived marine chart app
research.summary: The single most important architectural truth: you CANNOT composite Windy's animated weather layer on top of your own chart engine. The Windy Map Forecast API is a Leaflet 1.4.x library that creates and OWNS its own Leaflet map instance — Windy staff explicitly state "adding Map forecast API to a different leaflet is not possible." Embedding Windy therefore means using Windy's map as the base, which is incompatible with goal #1 (keep OpenCPN's own chart/quilting engine) and goals #3/#5 (composite satellite/Navionics charts + weather in one stack). Windy's Terms of Use additionally forbid caching/offline storage, mandate a permanent clickable Windy logo, and bar reconstructing raw model data — all of which clash with an offline-first marine app. The free tier is GFS-only and explicitly dev-only; production requires the Professional tier at 990 EUR/yr (+1,000 EUR for ECMWF). The honest, recommended path for goal #5 is to render your OWN weather layers from GRIB (GFS/ECMWF/NOAA via WeatherLayers GL or MapLibre/Mapbox raster-particle layers) so they composite natively over the OpenCPN chart — this is exactly what OpenCPN's existing GRIB plugin already does. Embed Windy, if at all, only as a separate optional "Windy mode" tab (its own full-screen map), never as your chart's weather overlay. Separately: PredictWind has NO public API (officially "on the development list," not available), so its routing cannot be embedded — you must build your own router over GRIB or license a routing engine. And the "select-an-area, fetch-on-demand" satellite/Navionics chart feature is the single biggest legal liability: Google and Bing tile ToS prohibit caching/offline storage, and converting Navionics charts (SAT2Chart-style) is a license violation; only Sentinel-2 (Copernicus open data) and, with caveats, ESRI World Imagery are legally clean sources to cache as mbtiles.

findings.claim: Windy's Map Forecast API OWNS its own Leaflet map instance — you cannot overlay its animated weather layer on your own chart engine.
findings.detail: It is a library based on Leaflet 1.4.x that calls windyInit() and instantiates/controls its own Leaflet map; it does not expose the weather/particle canvas as a detachable layer you can add to a pre-existing map. In the Windy community thread 'Adding Map-Forecast API weather overlays to a pre-existing Leaflet map,' Windy staff (Korina) state plainly: 'adding Map forecast API to a different leaflet is not possible.' A parallel thread ('how to render Windy Layer in existing Leaflet map of our app') has multiple developers asking the same and getting no working solution. CONSEQUENCE for Helm: embedding Windy = using Windy's map as the base layer, which is fundamentally incompatible with keeping OpenCPN's own chart/quilting engine and with compositing satellite/Navionics charts underneath the weather. Windy cannot be your in-chart weather overlay.

findings.claim: Windy Map Forecast API renders animated layers (wind, gust, rain, temp, pressure, waves, swell, currents) but only inside its own map, and the free tier is GFS-only and prohibited in production.
findings.detail: Layers include wind/gust, temperature, pressure, precipitation, clouds, waves, swell, ocean currents, plus isolines and a value picker (40+ layers on Professional). Tiers: Testing/free = GFS model only, layers limited to wind/temperature/pressure, 500 sessions/day, and 'development purpose only, not intended for production.' Professional = 990 EUR/yr (optional +1,000 EUR/yr for ECMWF, internal-use-only restricted), 10,000 sessions/day (raisable by agreement), ICON/NAM/AROME/HRRR/etc. So any shipped product needs the paid Professional tier, and ECMWF carries extra cost plus a redistribution restriction.

findings.claim: Windy's Terms of Use forbid caching/offline storage, require a permanent clickable Windy logo, and bar reconstructing raw model data — all hostile to an offline-first marine app.
findings.detail: The Specific Terms of Use for Windy Map & Point Forecast Services prohibit storing, extracting, modifying, redistributing the weather data or building derived databases; prohibit offline use and redistribution; require the Windy logo to remain unscaled, 100% opacity, clickable, linking to windy.com, plus 'Contains data from the Windy database' attribution; and forbid attempts to reconstruct original meteorological model data. For a navigation app that must work offline at sea and wants a native-looking, unbranded chart, these are blocking constraints — Windy can only be a live, online, branded view.

findings.claim: Windy Point Forecast API returns raw JSON point data only (no tiles, no layers, no map) — useful for a data readout, not for rendering weather over the chart.
findings.detail: It returns timestamped arrays for a single lat/lon (temp, wind, gust, precip, CAPE, clouds, RH, pressure, visibility; marine: wave height/period/direction, wind-wave, swell components, ocean + tidal currents; plus air quality) from GFS/ICON and regional/wave models. It is explicitly 'not tiles' — no raster/animated layers and no visualization. So you cannot get Windy's animated RASTER layers as data to draw yourself; the only thing Windy exposes as raw data is single-point forecasts.

findings.claim: The honest, native-compositing alternative is to render your OWN weather layers from GRIB (GFS/ECMWF/NOAA) — exactly what OpenCPN already does, and what WeatherLayers GL / MapLibre / Mapbox raster-particle layers enable on a modern engine.
findings.detail: OpenCPN's own GRIB plugin already ingests GFS/HRRR/ECMWF/RTOFS and draws wind barbs, isobars, isotachs, waves, current arrows directly on its charts — so the 'render your own' capability is native to the lineage you're forking. For a modern cross-platform engine: WeatherLayers GL is a commercial visualization layer (Raster/Contour/Particle/Grid) that integrates with deck.gl, MapLibre, Mapbox, Leaflet, OpenLayers from NOAA GFS/HRRR, ECMWF IFS/AIFS, Meteo-France, Copernicus ocean (waves/currents) — it composites ON your own base map. Mapbox GL JS and GL Native (mobile) added animated raster-particle layers for wind/wave/current magnitude+direction; MapLibre supports custom WebGL layers for the same. PROS vs Windy: composites natively over your OpenCPN chart, no Windy logo, works offline (GRIB cached on device), you control models/styling, multi-source, satellite-friendly. CONS: you build/maintain the GRIB ingest + rendering (or pay WeatherLayers), and you source/host the GRIB data yourself.

findings.claim: PredictWind has NO public API — its weather routing / departure planning cannot be embedded.
findings.detail: PredictWind's own help center FAQ states an API is 'not currently available' and only 'on the development list for the future,' prioritized by customer requests, no ETA. Existing integrations (Raymarine, Iridium) are hardware/app partnerships, not an open developer API. Therefore goal #4 (overlay PredictWind weather routing) is NOT achievable via PredictWind. Realistic options: (a) build your own isochronal weather router over the same GRIB you already ingest (the OpenCPN Weather Routing plugin is a working open-source reference), or (b) pursue a commercial license/partnership with a routing provider. Do not promise 'PredictWind routing' in-product.

findings.claim: The 'select-an-area, fetch-on-demand' satellite/Navionics chart feature is the biggest legal liability: Google/Bing prohibit caching tiles, and converting Navionics is a license violation.
findings.detail: Google Map Tiles API policy: 'must not pre-fetch, index, store, or cache any Content' except narrowly permitted — i.e., packaging Google satellite tiles into offline mbtiles is prohibited. Bing/Microsoft imagery terms are similarly restrictive on bulk caching/redistribution. Navionics-derived rasters (the SAT2Chart workflow) are a violation of Navionics' license — community sources note converting Navionics charts 'is illegal and will not be officially endorsed.' ChartLocker itself avoids the live-fetch model: it ships PRE-BUILT regional mbtiles ZIPs (Bing/Google/ArcGIS/Navionics) with safety-only disclaimers and no licensing claims — i.e., it operates in a grey/tolerated zone, not a licensed one. Building the LIVE on-demand fetcher productizes and scales that liability, which is far riskier than a hobbyist ZIP.

findings.claim: Only Sentinel-2 (Copernicus open data) is cleanly licensed to cache/redistribute as mbtiles; ESRI World Imagery is a possible licensed path; Google/Bing/Navionics are not.
findings.detail: Copernicus Sentinel data is free and open with no restrictions on commercial or non-commercial use, and EU law explicitly grants reproduction, distribution, adaptation, and combination — i.e., you may legally cache Sentinel-2 imagery into offline mbtiles, with attribution 'Contains modified Copernicus Sentinel data [Year].' Trade-off: ~10 m resolution, cloud-masking/compositing work, less useful for close-in pilotage than high-res aerials. ESRI World Imagery has its own terms requiring direct review for offline/redistribution (often allowed under an ArcGIS subscription/basemap terms) — viable as a licensed source but verify per-use. Practical recommendation: build the 'select-an-area, fetch, cache offline mbtiles' feature on Sentinel-2 (clean) plus official government raster/vector charts (NOAA ENC/RNC, etc., which are public-domain), and treat Bing/Google/Navionics as user-supplied, bring-your-own data you do NOT fetch/host.

research.recommendation: RENDER YOUR OWN GRIB-BASED WEATHER LAYERS as the product's primary weather system; do NOT make Windy your in-chart overlay. Windy cannot composite onto your OpenCPN chart (its API owns the Leaflet map — staff-confirmed impossible), its ToS bars caching/offline and forces a permanent clickable logo, and the free tier is GFS-only/dev-only with production gated behind a 990 EUR/yr Professional license. Build wind/gust/rain/temp/pressure/waves/swell/currents from GFS + ECMWF + NOAA GRIB using WeatherLayers GL or MapLibre/Mapbox raster-particle layers (this is exactly what OpenCPN's existing GRIB plugin already does, so it preserves goal #1 and composites natively over your chart, works offline, and carries no Windy branding). If you still want Windy, ship it ONLY as an optional, online-only, separate 'Windy mode' tab that loads Windy's own full-screen map with the required logo — never as your chart's weather layer; license the Professional tier before shipping. For routing (goal #4): drop the PredictWind embed (no public API exists); instead build an isochronal router over your own GRIB (the open-source OpenCPN Weather Routing plugin is your reference) or license a commercial routing engine — and do not market it as 'PredictWind.' For on-demand chart acquisition (goal #3): keep the ChartLocker-style select-area-fetch-mbtiles UX, but legally re-base it: fetch/cache only Sentinel-2 (Copernicus open data, clean to redistribute), official government charts (NOAA ENC/RNC, public domain), and optionally an ESRI World Imagery subscription tier; treat Bing/Google/Navionics as user-supplied bring-your-own layers you do not fetch or host, because caching Google/Bing tiles violates their ToS and converting Navionics violates its license. Net: one native engine, your own GRIB weather + your own router + legally-clean cached charts; Windy and PredictWind become optional online add-ons, not load-bearing dependencies.
constraints: Windy Map Forecast API instantiates and owns its own Leaflet 1.4.x map — it cannot be added as a layer to your existing chart map (Windy staff: 'adding Map forecast API to a different leaflet is not possible').

Windy free/Testing tier is GFS-only, ~3 layers, 500 sessions/day, and explicitly NOT permitted in production; production requires Professional at 990 EUR/yr, +1,000 EUR/yr for ECMWF (internal-use restricted).

Windy ToS forbids caching/storing/offline use and redistribution of weather data, forbids reconstructing raw model data, and mandates a permanent unscaled 100%-opacity clickable Windy logo + attribution — incompatible with an unbranded offline-first nav app.
Windy Point Forecast API is JSON point data only — no tiles, no animated layers, no map; it cannot supply Windy's raster layers as drawable data.
PredictWind has no public API (officially 'on the development list,' not available) — its weather routing/departure planning cannot be embedded; you must build or license your own router.

Google Map Tiles ToS prohibits pre-fetching/storing/caching tiles; Bing imagery similarly restricts bulk caching/redistribution — so Google/Bing satellite cannot be legally packaged into offline mbtiles by the app.

Navionics chart conversion (SAT2Chart-style raster extraction) violates Navionics' license; ChartLocker ships pre-built ZIPs in a tolerated grey zone and makes no licensing claims — productizing live on-demand fetch scales that liability.

Sentinel-2 (Copernicus) is the cleanly licensed satellite source (free, commercial OK, redistribution OK with 'Contains modified Copernicus Sentinel data [Year]' attribution) but is ~10 m resolution with cloud-handling overhead; ESRI World Imagery needs per-use terms review.

Native macOS/iPad/iPhone goal favors a single GL engine (Mapbox GL Native or MapLibre Native) with raster-particle weather — embedding Windy's web/Leaflet map would be a non-native webview that fights the native+touch requirement.

research.topic: Integrating "PredictWind weather routing" into the Helm cross-platform OpenCPN nav app: feasibility, ToS, and honest integration path
research.summary: PredictWind does NOT expose a public API for weather routing, forecasts, or GRIB — confirmed verbatim in their own FAQ ("PredictWind does not offer a public API"). Their optimal weather routing is computed in the PredictWind cloud (using their licensed AROME/ECMWF/UKMO/GFS blends) and delivered ONLY into their own Offshore app and OEM-embedded versions (Raymarine Lighthouse, Navico/B&G). There is no programmatic way for a third-party app to request "compute the optimal route for this boat." The ONLY sanctioned third-party hand-offs are: (1) GPX export of the user's already-computed optimal route from the Offshore app, and (2) GRIB1 file export of PredictWind forecast data. Therefore Helm cannot "call PredictWind routing." It must either (a) IMPORT the user's PredictWind-computed route as GPX and overlay it, and/or (b) build its OWN isochrone routing engine on GRIB + boat polars (the OpenCPN Weather Routing plugin model). The Windy weather-layer overlay (requirement #5) is a separate, genuinely licensable product — the Windy Map Forecast API — but its embeddable plugin is web/Leaflet-oriented and priced/limited per-session, which has real implications for a native iOS/macOS app.

findings.claim: PredictWind has NO public API — confirmed in their own words
findings.detail: PredictWind's official FAQ states verbatim: 'PredictWind does not offer a public API.' and 'While API access is not currently on our roadmap, it is something we may consider in the future as demand evolves.' The only programmatic offering they advertise is Map Embedding (embedding their weather MAPS on a website) — not data, not routing. This definitively rules out any 'call PredictWind to compute a route' integration. There is no documented, authorized REST/JSON endpoint for routing, forecasts, or GRIB retrieval by third parties.

findings.claim: Weather routing is computed in the PredictWind CLOUD and delivered only into their own/OEM apps
findings.detail: PredictWind's docs: 'The PredictWind cloud-based weather routing algorithm uses the following combinations automatically for calculating each weather route and departure plan.' The route is computed server-side using their licensed high-res model blends (AROME 1.3km, ECMWF 9km + ensemble to 30 days, UKMO 2km, NAM 5km, GFS, PWG/PWE) and rendered into the Offshore app. The small output file is designed for low-bandwidth satellite/SSB delivery into THEIR app — not exposed as data. OEM 'integrations' (Raymarine, Navico/B&G) are PredictWind's OWN Offshore app embedded on the MFD (installed as free Lighthouse apps via microSD / NOS modules), not an API or data feed Helm could license.

findings.claim: The ONE sanctioned route hand-off is GPX export of the already-computed optimal route
findings.detail: The Offshore app can export a computed weather route as a GPX file: 'After completing a weather route, you can export it in a GPX format to your GPS or Navigation software.' Workflow: Sail/Power Routing Map -> Tables -> arrow to Export -> select route -> Export as GPX. The user can export the averaged 'use my waypoints' route or the active route. KEY LIMITATIONS: you can export a weather ROUTE but NOT a departure plan; the help docs do not guarantee per-leg timestamps/ETAs in the GPX (GPX is fundamentally a waypoint/track container, so wind-at-waypoint and time-along-route metadata may be lost). On tablet it offers 'copy to nav software'; on Mac/PC it saves a .gpx to disk to import. This is a manual, user-driven, one-shot export — not a live sync.

findings.claim: The other sanctioned hand-off is GRIB (GRIB1) file export of PredictWind forecast data
findings.detail: PredictWind GRIB files cover models 'ECMWF, SPIRE, UKMO, GFS, PWG, PWE plus more regional models' with parameters 'Wind, Gust, Rain, Wave, Cloud, Temperature, Pressure and CAPE' (Professional adds ocean currents via Mercator/RTOFS, SST, hi-res tidal currents). The Offshore app can export GRIB1-type files for third-party chartplotters that read GRIB1. So Helm could ingest a user-exported PredictWind GRIB and display it — but a paid Standard/Professional subscription is required to download GRIBs over satellite/SSB and to use routing/departure tools. Note the DataHub does NOT pipe GRIB or routing to third-party apps; it only shares the boat's NMEA2000/AIS data over Wi-Fi as NMEA0183 (which OpenCPN/Navionics/AquaMaps can consume) and provides internet connectivity.

findings.claim: Building our OWN routing engine on GRIB + polars is the proven, license-clean path — the OpenCPN model
findings.detail: The OpenCPN Weather Routing plugin already does exactly this and is open source: it 'creates optimized weather routes using isochrone method and predictive grib data... and boat performance, calculated using boat specs or polar data.' Isochrone interval configurable (1/3/6/12h), with limits on wind speed and wave height, and reads GRIB1/GRIB2 plus climatology for long passages. A second open-source reference is gweatherrouting (multi-point routing, integrated boat/polar DB, GRIB1/2 downloaders for NOAA/OpenSkiron). Academic reference: VISIR-2 (Python ship-routing). Since Helm is built on OpenCPN and must keep all OpenCPN functionality additively, the Weather Routing + GRIB + Climatology plugins are ALREADY available to inherit — Helm gets isochrone routing 'for free' as a built-in, computed locally on user-supplied/free GRIB (NOAA GFS) with no PredictWind dependency.

findings.claim: Any routing engine REQUIRES boat polars (boat-performance curves) as input
findings.detail: Polars (boat speed as a function of true wind speed and true wind angle) are mandatory for meaningful weather routing. PredictWind itself enforces this: 'You must set the boat polars up in your PredictWind account before using the weather routing... on any of the PredictWind platforms.' Options: pick from a library of stock designs, simple cruising 'Sail Polars', or AI-generated polars (Professional + DataHub required). For Helm's own engine, the same input is needed — ship a polar library + a simple polar editor, accept standard .pol/.csv polar files (OpenCPN-compatible), and optionally learn polars from the user's logged NMEA performance over time. Wave polars / motion modelling (roll, slamming) are a PredictWind Professional-only refinement that a v1 engine can omit.

findings.claim: PredictWind subscription tiers and account device-lockout constrain ANY integration
findings.detail: Tiers: a free app exists (limited viewing); Standard adds weather routing, departure planning, and offshore data over satellite; Professional adds hi-res GRIBs (1km/8km PWG/PWE), ocean-current GRIBs (Mercator/RTOFS), SST maps, hi-res tidal currents, in-app AIS over satellite, AI polars + over-the-horizon AIS (with DataHub). CRITICAL ToS CONSTRAINT: the license is for ONE person and CANNOT be shared — 'If you try to access PredictWind using a 5th device (or 5 IP addresses) within a 6-hour period, you will be logged out... and the system will trigger a password reset.' This means Helm must NEVER store/replay a user's PredictWind credentials server-side to fetch data on their behalf — that would trip the device/IP lockout and violate ToS. Integration must stay client-side and user-initiated (the user exports a file from their own logged-in Offshore app).

findings.claim: Windy weather LAYERS (requirement #5) are licensable via the Windy Map Forecast API — but with real native-app caveats
findings.detail: Windy offers a Map Forecast API (animated map layers/isolines), a Point Forecast API (20+ params at a coordinate), and a Webcams API. Map Forecast pricing: a FREE 'Testing' tier (GFS only; wind/temp/pressure layers; ~500 sessions/day; explicitly 'development purpose only, not for production') and a PROFESSIONAL tier at ~EUR 990/yr (+EUR 1,000 for ECMWF) giving 40+ layers INCLUDING wind, gusts, rain, waves, currents and ~10,000 sessions/day (negotiable). Older quotes cite USD 720/yr / 5,000 unique visitors/day. KEY CAVEAT: the Map Forecast product is a Leaflet/web plugin — it's built to embed weather layers into a WEB map, and is metered per session/visitor, which fits a webview but is awkward and potentially costly for a fully native macOS/iOS Metal/MapKit chart engine. For a native chart canvas, the cleaner technical fit may be Windy's Point Forecast API for data, or sourcing the SAME underlying free models (NOAA GFS, GFS-Wave, RTOFS currents) directly as GRIB/tiles and rendering them in Helm's own engine — avoiding per-session metering and giving full offline caching.

findings.claim: There is no honest way to brand a non-PredictWind route as 'PredictWind weather routing'
findings.detail: Because routing is cloud-locked to PredictWind's own apps, if Helm computes a route with its OWN isochrone engine on free GRIB, it is NOT PredictWind's optimal route and must not be labelled as such — doing so would be inaccurate and a trademark/positioning risk. The only thing Helm can truthfully label 'PredictWind' is a route the user genuinely exported from their PredictWind Offshore app (imported as GPX) or PredictWind GRIB the user exported. This is the central honesty constraint on how the feature is surfaced in the UI.

constraints: PredictWind has NO public API for routing, forecasts, or GRIB — confirmed verbatim in their FAQ. Do not architect against an endpoint that does not exist.

Weather routing is computed server-side in the PredictWind cloud and delivered only into PredictWind's own Offshore app and OEM-embedded copies of it (Raymarine Lighthouse, Navico/B&G). 'Raymarine/B&G integration' is PredictWind's own app on the MFD, NOT a licensable data/API feed.

The ONLY user-initiated, sanctioned hand-offs are: (a) GPX export of an already-computed optimal route, and (b) GRIB1 file export of PredictWind forecast data — both manual, one-shot, client-side, requiring the user's own paid subscription.

Account license is single-person; 5 devices or 5 IPs within 6 hours triggers logout + forced password reset. NEVER store or replay user PredictWind credentials server-side to fetch data — it violates ToS and trips the lockout.
Routing of any kind requires boat polars as a mandatory input; ship a polar library + editor and accept standard polar files.
DataHub does NOT serve GRIB or routing to third-party apps; it only shares the boat's NMEA2000/AIS as NMEA0183 over Wi-Fi and provides internet connectivity.

Windy Map Forecast layers: free tier is GFS-only, 3 layers, ~500 sessions/day, explicitly NOT for production. The waves/currents/gust layers require the paid tier (~EUR 990/yr +ECMWF) and are metered per session — a poor fit for an unmetered native offline chart engine; verify current commercial/native-embedding terms directly with Windy.

Truth-in-labelling: only a route/GRIB the user actually exported from PredictWind may carry the 'PredictWind' label. A route computed by Helm's own engine must be labelled as Helm's own, not PredictWind's.
research.recommendation:
Surface "PredictWind weather routing" in Helm as a two-track feature, and be explicit in the UI about which track produced any given route.

TRACK A — "Import from PredictWind" (the honest, low-effort, ship-first path). Add a first-class import that ingests (1) the GPX optimal route the user exports from their own PredictWind Offshore app, and (2) PredictWind GRIB1 files. Render the imported route as a distinct, branded overlay ("PredictWind optimal route — imported"), draw the GRIB as a weather layer, and show departure-window context where the GPX carries it. On macOS use the system file picker / drag-drop; on iPad/iPhone register Helm as a handler for .gpx and .grb so PredictWind's "copy to nav software" share-sheet lands the route directly in Helm. This requires ZERO PredictWind business relationship, breaks no ToS, and is the only way to truthfully say "your PredictWind route, in Helm." Keep it client-side and user-initiated — never proxy their login.

TRACK B — "Helm Weather Routing" (the owned, differentiating engine). Inherit/keep the OpenCPN Weather Routing + GRIB + Climatology plugins (mandated by requirement #1's additive constraint), and put a modern, native, touch-first UI on top of them: select start/end, pick a polar from a built-in library or import one, choose a GRIB source, and compute an isochrone-optimal route + departure planner LOCALLY. Default the GRIB to free NOAA models (GFS, GFS-Wave, RTOFS currents) so this works with no paid subscription and fully offline. Label this clearly as Helm's own route — never as PredictWind's.

For requirement #5 (Windy LAYERS), keep it architecturally separate from routing. Prototype with Windy's Map Forecast API in a webview to validate the look, but plan the production layer as Helm's own renderer over the same free underlying models (GFS / GFS-Wave / RTOFS), so wind/gust/rain/waves/currents tiles are unmetered, offline-cacheable as mbtiles, and consistent with the on-demand chart-acquisition design (requirement #3). Treat the paid Windy tier as an optional "premium layers" toggle, and confirm its native-app + commercial-redistribution terms with Windy before committing.

Net: position the headline feature as "Bring your PredictWind route — or let Helm route you." Track A gives instant, honest PredictWind compatibility; Track B gives a no-subscription, offline, owned capability you fully control. Do NOT promise or build against a PredictWind routing API — it does not exist and is not on their roadmap.

research.topic: Feasibility of Helm: native cross-platform (macOS/iPad/iPhone) re-implementation of OpenCPN with on-demand chart fetch, PredictWind routing, embedded Windy layers
research.summary: OpenCPN cannot be ported to iOS. It is a wxWidgets/C++ GPLv2-or-later desktop app running only on Windows, macOS, Linux, and Android. Its GPL license, serial/USB I/O, and mouse-driven UI all conflict with the iOS sandbox and touch model. So OpenCPN on iPad means a clean re-implementation that PRESERVES the feature set, not a literal port. This is proven viable: qtVlm (Qt) and Aqua Map already ship native iOS chartplotters with ENC charts, GRIB routing, AIS with CPA/TCPA, and anchor alarms, fed over WiFi/TCP/UDP/SignalK/BLE. Reusable from OpenCPN: the open standards it implements (S-57/S-52, BSB/KAP, mbtiles, GPX, NMEA, GRIB) plus OpenCPN as a functional spec; the UI must be rebuilt natively. On the three headline features: on-demand satellite mbtiles is easy with GDAL but only Sentinel-2/Copernicus is cleanly licensed for fetch-cache-offline (Bing, Google, Navionics all forbid scraping/caching/offline); PredictWind has NO public API per its own FAQ (only web map-embedding); Windy is Leaflet web-only, forbids offline caching, and needs a paid Professional plan for production, so live Windy layers work only as an online web-view overlay.

findings.claim: OpenCPN full feature set is documented and must be preserved 1:1.
findings.detail: Charts: S-57 and S-63 ENC plus CM93 vector; BSB and KAP raster; mbtiles; quilting (seamless same-scale composite, Q-key toggle); chart groups; north-up and course-up. Routes, marks, tracks with GPX. AIS with CPA and TCPA, collision and proximity alarms, SART and MOB by MMSI. Tides and currents from harmonic data such as NOAA and XTide. GRIB and GRIB2 plugin. Dashboard instruments. NMEA 0183 and 2000 incl. SignalK over serial, USB, TCP, UDP; autopilot output. Anchor watch; MOB tracking. Over 45 plugins including radar and weather. Day, Dusk, Night palettes via the S-52 presentation library.

findings.claim: OpenCPN cannot run natively on iOS; OpenCPN on iPad must be a re-implementation.
findings.detail: It is wxWidgets/C++, in production only on Windows, macOS, Linux, and Android, and the Android port alone took years. Three blockers: GPLv2-or-later conflicts with App Store non-transferable terms (the VLC App Store problem); the iOS sandbox forbids raw serial and USB and arbitrary background; the desktop mouse-and-menu UI is not touch. All community OpenCPN-on-iPad answers are VNC or remote-desktop workarounds, not native apps.

findings.claim: Reuse the open standards and OpenCPN-as-spec, not the wxWidgets UI; native iOS chartplotters with this feature set already exist.
findings.detail: Practical reuse: open standards (S-57/S-52, NMEA 0183/2000, SignalK, GRIB, mbtiles, GPX) plus permissive libraries such as GDAL, PROJ, and an open S-57 parser; OpenCPN GPL source as reference only, not statically linked into a closed App Store binary. Must rebuild: UI, touch gestures, connectivity. Precedent: qtVlm is Qt and native on iOS and iPad doing ENC plus raster, GRIB routing, AIS, instruments; Aqua Map is native iOS doing ENC, AIS collision alerts, anchor alarm, wind/wave/current forecasts. Both are fed by WiFi gateways over TCP/UDP/SignalK.

findings.claim: On-demand satellite mbtiles is easy technically, but only Sentinel-2 is cleanly licensed.
findings.detail: Pipeline: select a bounding box, fetch imagery, build mbtiles with GDAL via gdal_translate plus gdaladdo or gdal2tiles or gdal2mbtiles, then cache offline. Sentinel-2 and Copernicus are free, open, commercial-OK, redistribution-OK, attribution required, and are the only source you can build fetch-and-cache on. Google Tile API forbids pre-fetch, caching, and offline, and forbids substantially similar products; Bing is similar; the Navionics Web API terms forbid reverse engineering and forbid accessing tiles outside the official API, with offline gated to a subscription. ChartLocker Bing, Google, and Navionics mbtiles are a tolerated personal-use gray area, not productizable.

findings.claim: PredictWind has no public API; routing cannot be overlaid as data.
findings.detail: The PredictWind FAQ states PredictWind does not offer a public API and that an API is not currently on the roadmap. They offer only Map Embedding of their web maps. So you cannot pull their optimal route or departure isochrones onto your chart. Realistic path: build your own GRIB-based isochrone routing engine since GRIB is open and is the same forecast substrate, which qtVlm already does, and treat PredictWind at most as an optional embedded web view.

findings.claim: Live Windy layers are feasible only as an online web overlay under a paid license.
findings.detail: The Windy Map Forecast API is a Leaflet 1.4.x JavaScript library for your website with no native iOS path, so on iPad and iPhone it runs in a web view. It is online-only by design with no offline or cache path, heavy localStorage use, and one map per page; the free Testing tier is forbidden in production so production needs paid Professional; full marine layers and models such as iconWave and CMEMS currents are paid. So embedded Windy works online with a paid license but not offline underway. For offline weather on the chart, render GRIB yourself; Stormglass and Xweather are licensable native marine-data alternatives.

findings.claim: iOS forces a connectivity rebuild: no serial or USB; WiFi, TCP, UDP, SignalK, BLE instead.
findings.detail: OpenCPN serial and USB NMEA inputs do not exist on stock iOS. The App-Store-blessed pattern used by Aqua Map, NMEAconnect, and NMEAremote receives NMEA 0183, 2000, and SignalK over WiFi via TCP or UDP, or over MQTT or BLE, from gateways such as Yacht Devices YDWG-02, Digital Yacht, Actisense W2K-1, Vesper, and B&G, plus internal GPS; autopilot output goes back over the network. Workable but net-new code.

constraints: PRESERVE: vector charts S-57 ENC, S-63 encrypted ENC, CM93 legacy.
PRESERVE: raster charts BSB and KAP (RNC) and mbtiles (raster and vector).
PRESERVE: chart quilting (seamless same-scale composite, user toggle).
PRESERVE: chart groups (scope charts by path or region).
PRESERVE: north-up and course-up orientation, lat/lon grid.
PRESERVE: S-52 rendering with Day, Dusk, Night palettes.
PRESERVE: routes, marks/waypoints, tracks with GPX import and export.
PRESERVE: AIS target tracking, CPA and TCPA, collision and proximity alarms, guard zones.
PRESERVE: SART handling and MOB alarm with tracking (selectable MMSI).
PRESERVE: anchor watch and alarm.
PRESERVE: tides and currents prediction and display via NOAA and XTide harmonics.
PRESERVE: GRIB and GRIB2 weather and current display; additively, native GRIB isochrone routing.
PRESERVE: dashboard instruments (depth, speed, wind, heading, position).
PRESERVE: NMEA 0183 and NMEA 2000 incl. SignalK ingestion.
PRESERVE (re-platformed for iOS): TCP, UDP, BLE, SignalK, WiFi gateway, and internal GPS inputs; serial and USB only where the OS allows on macOS.
PRESERVE: autopilot output (NMEA steering; over the network on iOS).
PRESERVE: radar and weather plugin capabilities such as radar_pi, AISradar, oTCurrent, climatology.
PRESERVE: plugin extensibility so the 45-plus plugin ecosystem is not lost.
TOS/LEGAL: do not scrape, cache, or ship offline Bing, Google, or Navionics tiles; use official licensed online APIs or user-imported mbtiles only.
TOS/LEGAL: on-demand satellite fetch is cleanly licensable only for Sentinel-2 and Copernicus with attribution, NOAA ENC and RNC, and OSM-class open sources.
TOS/LEGAL: PredictWind has no public API; web map-embedding only, no integrated route data.
TOS/LEGAL: Windy embedding is web-only, online-only, and needs paid Professional for production; not usable offline.

LICENSE: do not statically link OpenCPN GPLv2-or-later source into a closed App Store binary; reuse open standards and treat OpenCPN as the functional spec, building on permissive libs such as GDAL, PROJ, and an open S-57/S-52 engine.
research.recommendation: REBUILD natively; REUSE the open standards and OpenCPN as the functional spec, not the wxWidgets binary. A literal iOS port is blocked three ways: GPLv2-or-later versus App Store terms, the sandbox forbidding serial and USB and arbitrary background, and the desktop UI not being touch. qtVlm and Aqua Map prove a native re-implementation can carry the full OpenCPN feature set on iPad and iPhone. Architecture: build the chart, render, and nav core on permissive open libraries such as GDAL, PROJ, an open S-57/S-52 engine, mbtiles, GPX, GRIB, and NMEA/SignalK, shared across macOS, iPad, and iPhone via a shared C++/Qt core with native SwiftUI and Metal front-ends, or full Qt. Use OpenCPN GPL source as reference only; do not statically link it into a closed App Store binary. Connectivity is net-new: WiFi, TCP, UDP, SignalK, BLE, and internal GPS, with autopilot output sent over the network. Set honest expectations on the three headline features. One, on-demand chart fetch: ship it for cleanly-licensed sources only such as Sentinel-2 and Copernicus with attribution, NOAA ENC and RNC, and OSM, via select-area to GDAL to mbtiles to offline cache. Do not scrape or cache Bing, Google, or Navionics tiles in a shipped product; offer user-imported mbtiles like the ChartLocker model and official licensed online-only APIs for those. This is the biggest legal exposure; gate behind counsel review before launch. Two, PredictWind: no public API exists, so do not promise overlaid optimal-route data. Build your own GRIB-based isochrone weather-routing engine as the real ownable feature; treat PredictWind as at most an optional embedded web view. Three, Windy: feasible only as an online web-view overlay under a paid Professional license with no offline and no free tier in production. For offline weather on the chart, the actual underway use case, render GRIB yourself; consider Stormglass or Xweather for native cacheable marine data. Net: the product is buildable and differentiated with a modern native UI plus Sentinel-2 on-demand charts plus native GRIB routing and weather, but two of the three named integrations, PredictWind data and offline Windy, and the Bing, Google, and Navionics tile sourcing, are NOT deliverable as specified and must be re-scoped before they enter any roadmap or marketing.

research.topic: Cross-platform architecture for "Helm" — a modern macOS + iPadOS + iOS marine chart plotter (modern OpenCPN), with on-demand chart acquisition, PredictWind routing, and Windy weather layers
research.summary: A shared C++ core + native SwiftUI/Metal UI per Apple platform is the right architecture, because it (a) lets you reuse OpenCPN's GPL C++ navigation/S-57 engine, (b) hits 60fps on a map-heavy, sensor-connected, offline-first app, and (c) looks native on macOS/iPad/iPhone. The chart renderer should be MapLibre GL Native (iOS 6.0 ships a production Metal backend; renders raster+vector mbtiles and PMTiles offline) for the modern base/satellite/Navionics-raster layers, with OpenCPN's own S-52/S-57 canvas retained (as a GPL component or via GDAL) for true ENC display — MapLibre cannot render S-57/S-52 nautical symbology. On the data-source feasibility front there are hard, load-bearing constraints the user must internalize: (1) PredictWind has NO public API and explicitly says it won't build one — routing can only be obtained via GRIB ingestion or unofficial means, NOT a supported overlay. (2) Windy's API is web/Leaflet-only, prohibits offline caching of weather data, and bans direct-competitor/navigation use without "added value" — embedding live Windy layers natively and offline is largely non-compliant; the compliant path is open GRIB (GFS/ECMWF) rendered in your own engine. (3) The "select-an-area, fetch satellite + Navionics-raster as mbtiles, cache offline" feature is technically straightforward but legally the hard part: Bing and Google Maps tiles explicitly prohibit caching/offline storage; Esri World Imagery needs a paid commercial license (and a separate "for Export" layer); Navionics' only public API is web-only and prohibits using it inside a native app or caching tiles. The only cleanly licensable satellite source for offline mbtiles is Sentinel-2 (Copernicus, free for commercial use with attribution). ChartLocker itself operates in a legal grey zone that a commercial product cannot copy. Precedent apps (Aqua Map, iNavX, TimeZero/TZ iBoat, SEAiq, Savvy Navvy) are all native or near-native and license charts through formal channels (NOAA ENC free; Navionics/NV-Charts/C-MAP via paid SDK/in-app-purchase deals) rather than scraping tiles.

findings.claim: Recommended stack: shared C++ core + native SwiftUI/Metal UI per Apple platform — beats Flutter, React Native, and Qt for this specific app.
findings.detail: This app is map-heavy (needs 60fps GPU rendering), offline-first, sensor-connected (NMEA/SignalK over TCP/UDP/BLE), and must reuse OpenCPN's existing C++ navigation logic. A shared C++/Rust core compiled into each app, with platform-native UI, is the documented pattern for exactly this profile. SwiftUI compiles to native binaries and benchmarks fastest on iOS rendering vs RN and Flutter, and gives proper macOS look-and-feel (menus, multi-window, trackpad) that Flutter/RN/Qt approximate poorly. React Native is disqualified: its JS bridge/JSI adds overhead that hurts graphics-intensive and data-heavy work — wrong for a real-time chart canvas. Flutter is viable (Impeller renderer now gives consistent 60fps, Skia removed on iOS from 3.29) and would be the choice if a single shared UI codebase were the priority over native polish, but it renders its own non-native widgets, weakening the 'native on macOS' goal, and still needs C++ FFI for the nav core. Qt is C++-native and high-performance but is a desktop-first toolkit with weak/awkward iOS/iPadOS App Store ergonomics and non-native UI. Net: the core nav engine is C++ regardless; only the UI layer is the real decision, and native SwiftUI wins the stated 'modern + native on macOS, plus touch iPad/iPhone' goal. MapLibre Native (the renderer) is itself C++ with a Metal backend, so it slots cleanly under either a native or Flutter shell.

findings.claim: Chart renderer: MapLibre GL Native is production-ready on iOS/macOS with a Metal backend and renders raster+vector mbtiles/PMTiles offline — but it CANNOT render S-57/S-52 nautical charts.
findings.detail: MapLibre Native is an open-source C++ library with GPU acceleration via OpenGL/Metal/Vulkan; iOS 6.0.0 shipped a stable Metal renderer (Jan 2024 announcement, distributed via Swift Package Manager and CocoaPods). It renders both raster and vector tiles from local MBTiles files and has built-in PMTiles support, making it ideal for the offline satellite/raster base layers and Navionics-derived raster mbtiles. The critical gap: MapLibre uses the Mapbox Style Spec and has no concept of S-52 symbology (the IHO standard that defines how each S-57 ENC object is drawn — symbol, color, line style, safety contours). So for 'keep all OpenCPN functionality,' true ENC vector display must come from OpenCPN's own chart canvas (its S-57/S-52/CM93 engine) or a GDAL/OGR-based S-57 reader plus a custom symbology renderer — not from MapLibre. Practical hybrid: MapLibre for modern base maps, satellite, weather raster overlays, and Navionics-raster; OpenCPN's GPL canvas (or NOAA's pre-rendered ENC raster mbtiles from the NOAA Chart Display Service) for ENC. NOAA NCDS ships free, weekly-updated raster mbtiles derived from ENC data with paper-chart symbology — these drop straight into MapLibre and sidestep the live S-52 rendering problem for US waters.

findings.claim: PredictWind has NO public API and explicitly will not build one — overlaying its optimal route is not a supportable integration.
findings.detail: PredictWind's own FAQ states verbatim: 'PredictWind does not offer a public API. Our current business model is focused on providing a complete business-to-consumer platform,' and 'API access is not currently on our roadmap.' Their only third-party offering is map EMBEDDING on a website, not a routing API. Therefore Feature 4 ('overlay the PredictWind optimal route + departure planning') cannot be built against PredictWind directly. Options: (a) Ingest PredictWind GRIB files the user downloads via the PredictWind Offshore App (free, supports GFS/ECMWF/UKMO/ICON + PWG/PWE, variables wind/gust/rain/wave/cloud/temp/pressure/CAPE) and display them — but that is GRIB display, not PredictWind's proprietary route. (b) Build your own routing engine on open GRIB + boat polars (this is what gives you a defensible, license-clean optimal-route feature). (c) Treat PredictWind as out of scope and integrate an API-available router. Honest bottom line: a native 'PredictWind route overlay' is not feasible through any sanctioned channel today.

findings.claim: Windy's API is web/Leaflet-only and its ToS prohibits offline caching and direct-competitor/navigation use — embedding live Windy layers natively-and-offline is largely non-compliant.
findings.detail: The Windy Map Forecast API is 'a simple-to-use library based on Leaflet 1.4.x'; the ToS states 'The API is designed for use in a web browser and utilizes Javascript, HTML and CSS' — it is not a native SDK, so native use would require a WebView and explicit clearance from api@windy.com. The ToS prohibits 'store, extract, modify, distribute, use the weather data' and forbids 'create any weather works or databases derived therefrom' — i.e., NO offline caching of Windy layers, which directly conflicts with the offline-first requirement. It also bars 'direct competition' and requires 'significant added value,' and you 'cannot implement the Services solely in the paid part' of your app. Pricing: free Testing tier (500 sessions/day, GFS + 3 layers, dev-only); Professional is ~EUR 990/year (10,000 sessions/day) plus ~EUR 1,000 for ECMWF, with ECMWF 'for internal use only.' Honest bottom line: a marine nav app is plausibly 'direct competition,' the layers can't be cached offline, and it's web-only — so live embedded Windy layers are at best an online-only WebView feature pending Windy's written approval. The compliant, offline-capable alternative is to render open GRIB (GFS free; ECMWF open data) with your own particle/heatmap layers in MapLibre/Metal — giving you the same wind/gust/rain/wave/current visuals you control and can cache.

findings.claim: On-demand 'select an area, fetch satellite + Navionics raster, package as mbtiles, cache offline' is technically easy but the imagery licensing is the hard blocker — only Sentinel-2 is cleanly licensable for offline commercial mbtiles.
findings.detail: Per-source ToS reality for caching tiles into offline mbtiles in a commercial app: (1) BING MAPS — 'download and usage of Bing map tiles besides the Bing services is prohibited'; offline storage violates the license. NOT usable. (2) GOOGLE MAPS PLATFORM — ToS explicitly forbids 'pre-fetch, index, store, reshare, or rehost' and 'bulk download Google Maps tiles'; only place_id/lat-long values are cacheable, not imagery. NOT usable. (3) ESRI/ArcGIS WORLD IMAGERY — the standard layer 'is not intended to be used to export tiles for offline'; Esri provides a separate 'World Imagery (for Export)' layer, and offline/commercial use requires a paid commercial license with downstream-customer license agreements and audit rights; 'programmatic use of session tokens (e.g., exporting volumes of basemap tiles) is not permitted.' Usable ONLY under a paid Esri commercial agreement. (4) SENTINEL-2 (Copernicus) — fully free for commercial AND non-commercial use under the Copernicus open license, attribution 'Contains modified Copernicus Sentinel data [YEAR]'; available via Copernicus Data Space, AWS Open Data, Google Earth Engine, MS Planetary Computer. This is the one clean source you can tile-on-demand and cache as mbtiles. (5) NAVIONICS — the only public 'Web API' (now Garmin) is, per its ToS, browser/web-only, must not run only behind a firewall, and is a tile-serving web product, NOT a license to embed charts in a native app or cache them offline; Navionics-in-your-app requires a separate commercial cartography/SDK deal (the path iNavX/X-Traverse and others use via formal IAP licensing). ChartLocker's free Navionics-derived raster mbtiles are a hobbyist grey-area redistribution that a commercial product cannot lawfully replicate. Honest bottom line: build the 'select-area → fetch → mbtiles → offline cache' pipeline on Sentinel-2 (and free NOAA ENC/RNC raster), add Esri imagery under a paid Esri license if you want sharper coastal imagery, and license Navionics/C-MAP/NV-Charts through formal cartography agreements — do NOT cache Bing/Google/Navionics-web tiles.

findings.claim: iOS marine-data reality: no serial/USB NMEA — data arrives over Wi-Fi TCP/UDP, SignalK (WebSocket/JSON), and BLE. SignalK is the modern standard to target.
findings.detail: iOS cannot take serial/USB NMEA directly; on a boat the data path is a Wi-Fi NMEA gateway (e.g., Digital Yacht WLN10, Yacht Devices YDWG-02) that broadcasts NMEA 0183/2000 over TCP or UDP (commonly port 10110), or a SignalK server. SignalK is JSON-over-WebSocket/HTTP designed exactly for phones/tablets, converting NMEA0183/2000/SeaTalk into a web-friendly model with a 'delta' update stream — it should be Helm's primary ingestion path, with raw TCP/UDP NMEA 0183 as fallback and BLE GPS for handheld use. Apps like NMEAremote and NMEAconnect prove the TCP/UDP/BLE/SignalK pattern on iOS. Architecturally: a connection-manager module in the C++ (or Swift) core that speaks SignalK delta + NMEA 0183 over TCP/UDP/BLE, normalizes to an internal model, and feeds the chart canvas/instruments.

findings.claim: Anchor watch needs background location; App Store will approve it but with strict justification, and iOS will suspend a poorly-built background app.
findings.detail: Anchor watch requires continuous background location. Apple's pattern: declare the location background mode, request 'Always' authorization with a clear purpose string, and expect review scrutiny that the background use is genuinely user-facing (anchor-drag alarm). Real shipping apps (Boat Beacon, multiple 'Anchor Alarm/Watch' apps) do this and surface the standard 'may use location in background, decreasing battery life' disclosure. Known pitfall reported by these apps: iOS can deprioritize/suspend a background app under memory pressure, so the anchor-watch loop must be implemented robustly (significant-location/region monitoring, local notifications for drag alerts) rather than naive continuous polling. This is a solvable, well-trodden constraint — not a blocker — but it must be designed for, and the 'Always' location justification is the most likely review friction point for a nav app.

findings.claim: Precedent apps confirm the architecture and the licensing model: all are native/near-native, render their own chart engine, and license cartography through formal channels — none scrape commercial tiles.
findings.detail: AQUA MAP (Geodata Engineering, Italy): native iOS/iPadOS + Apple-Silicon macOS; vector+raster offline charts; free NOAA data, Explorer Charts for the Bahamas, USACE survey data, ActiveCaptain/Waterway Guide overlays; supports importing custom MBTiles and raster overlays with opacity slider — the closest model to Helm's offline-mbtiles vision, sourcing free/licensed data, not scraped imagery. iNavX (+ Fugawi X-Traverse): the multi-source licensing exemplar — sells charts via in-app purchase from Navionics, CHS, NV Charts, nzMariner, Hydrographica, plus Theyr weather and AIS, all through formal X-Traverse deals; users also load their own GRIB. TIMEZERO / TZ iBoat: proprietary high-performance 2D/3D graphics engine ('TZ MAPS') that mixes vector + raster + satellite photo + bathymetry + weather overlays; auto-downloads free NOAA S-57; licenses C-MAP/MapMedia mm3d — shows the 'one engine, many overlays' target and that a custom GPU renderer is a viable (if expensive) alternative to MapLibre. SEAiq: native iOS, S-57/S-63 ENC focus, used by professional pilots — proves native S-52 ENC rendering on iOS is achievable. SAVVY NAVVY: modern consumer UX over official hydrographic + live weather + auto-routing (the 'Google-Maps-for-boats' UX bar Helm should match); stack is not publicly confirmed but it is mobile-native with vector tiles. 'Good' looks like: native-feeling, buttery 60fps pan/zoom; one engine mixing ENC + raster + satellite + weather with opacity; offline by default; multi-source licensed charts via IAP; clean connection to onboard instruments via SignalK/NMEA-over-WiFi.

findings.claim: Reusing OpenCPN code is possible but the GPL copyleft has commercial consequences that must be designed around.
findings.detail: OpenCPN is GPLv2+ (some parts LGPLv2+), C/C++ on wxWidgets with OpenGL. You may build a commercial product on it, but any distributed work that is a GPL derivative must itself be offered under GPLv2 with source available. Two clean patterns: (1) keep OpenCPN's GPL chart/S-57 engine as a separately-licensed open component (or use its plugin architecture) and keep your proprietary modern UI/cloud/IAP code at arm's length across a process or stable interface boundary to avoid the whole app becoming GPL; or (2) avoid OpenCPN code entirely and rebuild ENC rendering on GDAL/OGR (which reads S-57) + your own S-52 symbology — more work but no copyleft entanglement. The LGPL'd wxWidgets and the C++/OpenGL heritage also mean OpenCPN's renderer is OpenGL, not Metal, so direct canvas reuse on iOS means shipping OpenGL (deprecated but still functional on iOS) or porting — another reason MapLibre-Metal handles the modern layers while OpenCPN's engine is contained to ENC. Get IP/licensing counsel before committing to embedding OpenCPN source in a closed-source product.

research.recommendation: Build Helm as a shared C++/Rust core + native SwiftUI/Metal UI per Apple platform (iOS, iPadOS, macOS), with MapLibre GL Native (Metal backend) as the modern chart/overlay renderer for raster+vector mbtiles/PMTiles, satellite, and weather layers, and a contained OpenCPN-derived (GPL) or GDAL-based S-57/S-52 engine for true ENC display so 'all OpenCPN functionality' is preserved additively. Marine data ingestion: a core connection manager speaking SignalK (WebSocket/JSON delta) primary, plus raw NMEA 0183 over TCP/UDP and BLE GPS — there is no serial/USB on iOS. Anchor watch via background-location with 'Always' authorization, region/significant-location monitoring, and local-notification drag alerts. For the on-demand 'select-area → fetch → mbtiles → offline cache' feature, build the pipeline but source it lawfully: Sentinel-2 (free, commercial-OK, attribution) as the default satellite layer, free NOAA ENC/RNC raster mbtiles (NOAA Chart Display Service) for US charts, optional Esri 'World Imagery (for Export)' under a paid Esri commercial license for sharper coastal imagery, and Navionics/C-MAP/NV-Charts only through formal cartography/IAP deals (the iNavX/TimeZero model). Do NOT cache Bing, Google, or Navionics-web tiles — all three prohibit offline storage. Be explicit with the user that two requested features cannot be delivered as stated: PredictWind has no public API and won't build one (substitute: ingest user-downloaded PredictWind GRIB, or build your own GRIB+polars router), and Windy's API is web/Leaflet-only, bans offline caching, and likely bars a competing nav app (substitute: render open GFS/ECMWF GRIB as your own cacheable wind/gust/rain/wave/current layers in MapLibre/Metal — same visuals, you control and own them offline). Phase it: MVP = SwiftUI/MapLibre shell + NOAA ENC raster + Sentinel-2 on-demand mbtiles + SignalK/NMEA + GRIB weather; Phase 2 = OpenCPN S-52 ENC engine, anchor watch, own routing engine; Phase 3 = licensed Navionics/C-MAP and optional online-only Windy WebView if Windy grants written approval. Get IP counsel on the OpenCPN GPL boundary and a cartography-licensing lead before launch.

architecture.stackDecision: Shared C++ core + native Apple UIs (SwiftUI/Metal), NOT a port and NOT a cross-platform UI toolkit. OpenCPN cannot run on iOS (wxWidgets desktop UI, GPLv2-or-later vs App Store non-transferable terms, sandbox forbids serial/USB, mouse-driven UI not touch) — so iPad/iPhone are a clean re-implementation, not a port; only macOS can reuse OpenCPN's actual canvas. Therefore: one shared C++ "nav core" (chart access, S-57/S-52 ENC engine, GRIB parsing, isochrone routing, AIS/CPA-TCPA, tides/currents harmonics, anchor-watch logic, NMEA/SignalK connection manager, mbtiles/GPX/ENC I/O) compiled into all three targets, with platform-native front-ends: SwiftUI + Metal on iOS/iPadOS (touch-first), and SwiftUI/AppKit on macOS (menus, multi-window, trackpad, drag-drop, file pickers). Rejected alternatives, with reasons: React Native (JS bridge/JSI overhead is wrong for a 60fps real-time chart canvas — disqualified); Flutter (viable with Impeller at 60fps, but renders non-native widgets which undercuts the "native on macOS" goal, and still needs C++ FFI for the nav core anyway — so you pay the FFI cost without the native polish); Qt (C++-native and high-performance, would let you share UI too, but it is desktop-first with awkward iOS/iPadOS App Store ergonomics and non-native look — acceptable fallback if a single shared UI codebase ever outranks native polish, but it loses the stated goal). The nav core is C++ regardless of choice, so the only real decision is the UI layer, and native SwiftUI wins the explicit "modern + native on macOS, plus touch iPad/iPhone" requirement. GPL boundary: keep any OpenCPN-derived GPL code (most plausibly the S-52/S-57 ENC canvas) as a contained, arm's-length component behind a stable interface/process boundary — or rebuild ENC on GDAL/OGR (reads S-57) + a custom S-52 symbology renderer to avoid copyleft entanglement entirely in the closed App Store binary. Get IP counsel before embedding any OpenCPN source.
architecture.chartEngine: Hybrid two-renderer design — this is load-bearing, not a nicety. (1) MapLibre GL Native (Metal backend, production-ready on iOS/macOS as of MapLibre Native iOS 6.0) is the MODERN base/overlay renderer: it draws raster + vector mbtiles and PMTiles offline, GPU-accelerated, and handles the satellite layers (Sentinel-2), the on-demand-fetched mbtiles, NOAA raster mbtiles, OpenSeaMap seamarks, and the GRIB-derived weather raster/particle overlays. (2) A contained S-57/S-52 ENC engine for TRUE vector nautical charts, because MapLibre uses the Mapbox Style Spec and has NO concept of S-52 symbology (IHO standard for ENC symbols, colors, safety contours, Day/Dusk/Night palettes) — MapLibre fundamentally cannot render S-57/S-52. Two ways to get the ENC engine: (a) reuse OpenCPN's GPL S-52/S-57/CM93 canvas as a contained GPL component (OpenGL heritage; ship OpenGL on iOS, deprecated-but-functional, or port the canvas), or (b) build on GDAL/OGR's S-57 reader + your own S-52 symbology renderer (no copyleft, more work). Pragmatic shortcut for US waters and MVP: NOAA's Chart Display Service (NCDS) publishes free, weekly-updated raster mbtiles derived from ENC with paper-chart symbology — these drop straight into MapLibre and sidestep live S-52 rendering until Phase 2. Net: MapLibre for everything raster/satellite/weather/quilted-mbtiles; dedicated S-52 engine layered/composited for genuine ENC display so "keep all OpenCPN functionality" holds additively. TimeZero/TZ iBoat (custom GPU engine mixing vector+raster+satellite+bathymetry+weather) proves the "one engine, many overlays" target is achievable but expensive; MapLibre buys most of it for free.
architecture.onDemandChartPipeline: User lassos bbox + picks max zoom (cap Z16/17, opt-in Z18) -> server/edge tiler converts to XYZ tile list -> fetches source -> georeferences (gdalwarp+GCPs only for ad-hoc imagery; Sentinel-2/NOAA are already georeferenced) -> packs mbtiles via GDAL MBTiles driver / gdal2tiles (FLIP Y: mbtiles is TMS y=0-south vs XYZ y=0-north) -> device downloads packed mbtiles, caches offline, displays via MapLibre + quilting + Chart Groups. SHIPPABLE sources: Sentinel-2/Copernicus (free, commercial-OK, redistribute with attribution; 10m res), NOAA ENC + NCDS raster mbtiles (US public domain), OpenSeaMap (ODbL, seamarks overlay only). NOT shippable / BYO-only: Google + Bing (ToS bar caching; Bing also EOL 2028), Esri (gray — paid 'for Export' layer + Master License + 100k-tile cap only), Navionics (partnership-or-nothing; official SDK is online-only, scraping prohibited). Preserve the full ChartLocker workflow via 'import my own .mbtiles' (BYO) so the company never touches a prohibited tile.
