architecture.weatherLayerApproach: PRIMARY = own GRIB layers composited natively over the chart (offline, unbranded, you own it; same as OpenCPN's GRIB plugin); Windy only as an optional online WebView tab, never the chart overlay — because the Windy API owns its own Leaflet map (staff-confirmed it cannot be added to another map), forbids caching/offline, mandates a permanent logo, and is non-production on the free tier / ~EUR990+yr paid. Honest tradeoff: own-GRIB costs you the ingest+render build but is offline-capable, native-composite, and ToS-free; Windy is a richer live view but online-only, branded, metered, uncacheable, and possibly barred as 'direct competition.'
architecture.predictwindIntegration: PredictWind has NO public API — confirmed verbatim in their own FAQ ("PredictWind does not offer a public API," "not currently on our roadmap"). Their optimal weather routing + departure planning is computed server-side in the PredictWind cloud (licensed AROME/ECMWF/UKMO/GFS blends) and delivered ONLY into their own Offshore app and OEM-embedded copies (Raymarine Lighthouse, Navico/B&G — those are PredictWind's own app on the MFD, NOT a licensable feed). So a live "compute the optimal route for this boat" or "overlay the PredictWind route" integration is IMPOSSIBLE and must not be promised or built. Surface it as a two-track feature with the UI explicit about which track produced any given route: TRACK A — "Import from PredictWind" (ship-first, honest, zero business relationship, breaks no ToS): ingest the GPX optimal route the user EXPORTS from their own logged-in PredictWind Offshore app, plus PredictWind GRIB1 files they export; render the imported route as a distinctly-branded overlay ("PredictWind optimal route — imported") and draw the GRIB. On macOS use the file picker/drag-drop; on iPad/iPhone register Helm as a handler for .gpx and .grb so PredictWind's "copy to nav software" share-sheet lands it directly. CRITICAL ToS: keep it 100% client-side and user-initiated — NEVER store/replay the user's PredictWind credentials server-side to fetch on their behalf; their license is single-person and 5 devices/IPs within 6 hours triggers a forced logout + password reset. Note limits: you can export a route but NOT a departure plan, and GPX may lose per-leg ETA/wind metadata. TRACK B — "Helm Weather Routing" (the owned, differentiating engine): inherit OpenCPN's Weather Routing + GRIB + Climatology plugin model and put a modern touch-first UI on it — pick start/end, choose a boat polar (built-in library, simple editor, or imported .pol/.csv; polars are a MANDATORY input to any router), select a GRIB source (default free NOAA GFS/GFS-Wave/RTOFS so it works offline with no subscription), and compute an isochrone-optimal route + departure planner LOCALLY. TRUTH-IN-LABELLING: a Helm-computed route must be labelled Helm's own, never "PredictWind" — only a route/GRIB the user genuinely exported from PredictWind may carry that name (trademark/accuracy risk otherwise). Headline positioning: "Bring your PredictWind route — or let Helm route you."
architecture.nmeaConnectivity: iOS has NO serial/USB NMEA — connectivity is a net-new rebuild, not inherited from OpenCPN. The App-Store-blessed pattern (proven by Aqua Map, NMEAremote, NMEAconnect, qtVlm) ingests boat data over the network: a core connection-manager module in the shared C++ (or Swift) core that speaks SignalK (JSON-over-WebSocket "delta" stream — the modern primary target, since it normalizes NMEA0183/2000/SeaTalk for phones/tablets) as the primary path, with raw NMEA 0183 over TCP and UDP (commonly port 10110) as fallback, BLE for handheld GPS, and the device's internal GPS. Sources are Wi-Fi NMEA gateways (Digital Yacht WLN10, Yacht Devices YDWG-02, Actisense W2K-1, Vesper, B&G). The connection manager normalizes everything to one internal model that feeds the chart canvas, AIS engine (CPA/TCPA, collision/proximity alarms, guard zones, SART, MOB-by-MMSI), and dashboard instruments. Autopilot output (NMEA steering) goes back OVER THE NETWORK on iOS, not serial. On macOS only, serial/USB is allowed where the OS permits, in addition to the network paths. Anchor watch needs continuous background location: declare the location background mode, request "Always" authorization with a clear purpose string, and implement robustly with region/significant-location monitoring + local notifications for drag alerts (naive continuous polling gets suspended under memory pressure) — the "Always" justification is the most likely App Store review friction point and is well-trodden (Boat Beacon et al.).
architecture.offlineSync: Offline-first is the default state, not a mode — the app must be fully functional with zero connectivity underway. Data classes and their sync models: (1) CHARTS — mbtiles/ENC stored on-device; on-demand pipeline fetches+packs server-side, device downloads the packed mbtiles once and caches permanently; chart updates (NOAA weekly NCDS) pulled opportunistically when online, never required to navigate. (2) GRIB WEATHER — downloaded while online (or imported from PredictWind), cached on device, rendered offline; show forecast age/staleness prominently. (3) ROUTES/MARKS/TRACKS — authored offline, stored locally as GPX, optional cloud sync (iCloud/CloudKit or own backend) for cross-device continuity with last-write-wins or simple per-object versioning; GPX import/export is the interop format across all three platforms. (4) LIVE NMEA/AIS/position — real-time only, never "synced," consumed from the boat network; internal GPS as fallback. (5) Windy — explicitly NOT cacheable (ToS) and therefore online-only by design. Shared data formats across macOS/iPad/iPhone: mbtiles (raster+vector charts), ENC/S-57, GPX (routes), GRIB1/GRIB2 (weather), polars (.pol/.csv). The tiler/packaging server is the only required backend; everything else degrades gracefully to fully-local. Be explicit in UI about what is fresh vs cached vs stale (chart edition dates, GRIB run time, AIS last-seen).
architecture.windyIntegration: Optional, online-only, separate "Windy mode" tab — NEVER the chart's weather overlay. Technically forced: the Windy Map Forecast API is a Leaflet 1.4.x JS library that owns its own map instance (Windy staff: "adding Map forecast API to a different leaflet is not possible"), so on iPad/iPhone/macOS it can only run inside a WebView as Windy's own full-screen map, with its mandatory permanent clickable logo + "Contains data from the Windy database" attribution. Legally constrained: ToS forbids caching/offline/redistribution and reconstructing model data (so it cannot satisfy the offline requirement), bars direct-competition/no-added-value use (a nav app is plausibly "direct competition"), and the free tier is GFS-only and explicitly non-production. Commercially: production requires Professional ~EUR 990/yr (+~EUR 1,000 ECMWF, internal-use-restricted), metered per session/visitor (~10,000 sessions/day) — awkward and potentially costly for a native app. RECOMMENDATION: prototype with Windy to validate the look, but ship it only as an opt-in "premium online Windy view" tab gated behind the paid license and written clearance from api@windy.com; the product's real, ownable, offline weather is the own-GRIB renderer. Do not market Windy as offline or as compositing on the chart.
architecture.sharedCore: Shared C++ "nav core" compiled into all three Apple targets, containing everything that is platform-agnostic: chart access + quilting + Chart Groups; the S-57/S-52/CM93 ENC engine (contained GPL component or GDAL/OGR-based, behind a stable interface to firewall copyleft); raster/mbtiles/PMTiles access (via MapLibre Native, itself C++ with Metal); GRIB1/GRIB2 parsing + weather rendering data; the isochrone weather-routing engine + polar handling; AIS target tracking with CPA/TCPA, collision/proximity/guard-zone/SART/MOB logic; tides & currents harmonic prediction (NOAA/XTide); anchor-watch logic; the NMEA0183/2000/SignalK connection manager + normalization model; autopilot output; and GPX/ENC/mbtiles/polar/GRIB I/O. The on-demand TILER (bbox->XYZ->fetch->georef->mbtiles, with the TMS Y-flip) runs server/edge-side, not in the device core, so source credentials and source-swapping stay off-device. Native front-ends sit on top: SwiftUI+Metal touch UI on iOS/iPadOS, SwiftUI/AppKit on macOS (menus, multi-window, trackpad, drag-drop, file handlers for .gpx/.grb/.mbtiles). Reuse strategy: reuse the OPEN STANDARDS (S-57/S-52, NMEA, SignalK, GRIB, mbtiles, GPX, BSB/KAP) and OpenCPN as the FUNCTIONAL SPEC; reuse permissive libs (GDAL, PROJ, MapLibre Native, an open S-57 parser); use OpenCPN GPL source as reference, and if embedded at all keep it arm's-length — do NOT statically link GPLv2+ source into a closed App Store binary without IP counsel sign-off. Precedent that this works: qtVlm and Aqua Map already ship native iOS chartplotters carrying the full feature set (ENC, GRIB routing, AIS CPA/TCPA, anchor alarm) fed over Wi-Fi/SignalK.

phasedPlan.scope: IP/licensing counsel on the OpenCPN GPL boundary (contained component vs GDAL rebuild) BEFORE any code embeds GPL source; cartography-licensing lead; confirm Copernicus/NOAA/OpenSeaMap attribution obligations; written terms check with Windy (api@windy.com) and document that PredictWind has no API. Stand up the shared C++ core skeleton + build for macOS/iPadOS/iOS; pick MapLibre Native (Metal) and wire an empty SwiftUI shell on each platform.
phasedPlan.deliverables: Counsel memo on GPL/IP boundary + cartography path; shared-core build green on all 3 targets; MapLibre rendering a basic mbtiles on iPad; attribution/ToS guardrail register.

phasedPlan.phase: Phase 1 — MVP: native chart + safe on-demand charts + connectivity + own GRIB
phasedPlan.scope: SwiftUI/MapLibre shell on iPad/iPhone/macOS; NOAA ENC raster (NCDS) mbtiles + Sentinel-2 on-demand select-area->mbtiles->offline pipeline (server tiler with TMS Y-flip); BYO 'import my own .mbtiles' (the ChartLocker bridge); SignalK + NMEA0183-over-TCP/UDP + internal GPS connectivity; AIS with CPA/TCPA + alarms; routes/marks/tracks via GPX import/export; own-GRIB weather layers (GFS/GFS-Wave/RTOFS) composited over the chart, offline-cached. Mandatory 'supplemental, not for primary nav' disclaimer on satellite.
phasedPlan.deliverables: Shippable beta: native touch chartplotter, on-demand Sentinel-2 + NOAA charts cached offline, BYO mbtiles import, AIS, GPX routes, own offline GRIB weather, boat-network connectivity.

phasedPlan.phase: Phase 2 — True ENC + owned routing + anchor watch + PredictWind import
phasedPlan.scope: Contained S-52/S-57/CM93 ENC engine (GPL component or GDAL+custom S-52) with Day/Dusk/Night palettes, quilting, Chart Groups — full OpenCPN-grade vector display; isochrone weather-routing engine + polar library/editor (.pol/.csv) on free NOAA GRIB (Track B 'Helm Weather Routing'); anchor watch via background 'Always' location + region monitoring + drag notifications; tides/currents harmonics; dashboard instruments; PredictWind Track A — GPX route + GRIB1 import via file picker / iOS share-sheet handlers (.gpx/.grb), client-side only.
phasedPlan.deliverables: Full OpenCPN feature parity additively; owned offline isochrone router with polars; anchor watch; PredictWind route/GRIB import labelled honestly; tides/currents + instruments.

phasedPlan.phase: Phase 3 — Licensed premium charts + optional online Windy + plugin extensibility
phasedPlan.scope: Formal cartography deals for Navionics/C-MAP/NV-Charts via official SDK/IAP (the iNavX/X-Traverse model) as ONLINE or licensed-offline layers — never scraped; optional Esri 'World Imagery (for Export)' under paid Esri license for sharper coastal imagery within the cap; optional online-only 'Windy mode' WebView tab under Professional license + written Windy approval; plugin/extensibility architecture so the 45+ OpenCPN plugin ecosystem (radar, climatology, oTCurrent) can be carried forward; autopilot output over network.
phasedPlan.deliverables: Licensed premium charts via IAP; optional Esri + Windy online add-ons; plugin extensibility; commercial-launch-ready product with counsel sign-off on every source.

legalGuardrails: DO NOT scrape, bulk-download, cache, or ship offline Google Maps tiles — ToS explicitly bars pre-fetch/store/cache/bulk-download of imagery; PROHIBITED in a shipping product. Treat Google as user-BYO only, never server-fetch/host.
DO NOT build on Bing Maps imagery — not supported for offline use, and Bing Maps Enterprise is EOL June 30 2028 with no new licensees since June 2024. DO-NOT-BUILD.

Esri/ArcGIS World Imagery offline is GRAY/licensable ONLY via the separate paid 'World Imagery (for Export)' layer under the Esri Master License with a ~100,000-tile export cap, inside Esri's own SDK — not free redistribution; only enter under a signed paid Esri commercial agreement.

DO NOT scrape/rasterize Navionics web tiles (the ChartLocker method) — Navionics ToS forbids accessing tiles via any interface other than the official Maps API and forbids reverse engineering. The ONLY legitimate Navionics path is the approval-gated, paid Garmin/Navionics Web API or Mobile SDK, which is ONLINE-display-only — 'offline Navionics on-demand' is not offered even on the paid path.

Sentinel-2/Copernicus is SAFE for commercial use, redistribution, and derivatives but ONLY with attribution 'Copernicus Sentinel data [Year]' / 'Contains modified Copernicus Sentinel data [Year]'. Render attribution permanently. Disclose its 10m resolution limit.

NOAA ENC/RNC are US public domain (safe, bulk-OK; prefer ENC/NCDS as RNC tile/seamless services were retired 2021/2022). OpenSeaMap is ODbL (attribution + share-alike) — overlay only, lacks complete soundings, not a primary chart.

PredictWind has NO public API and won't build one — do NOT architect against a routing endpoint, do NOT promise an in-app PredictWind route overlay, and do NOT store/replay user PredictWind credentials server-side (single-person license; 5 devices/IPs in 6h triggers forced logout + password reset). Only a route/GRIB the user themselves exported may carry the 'PredictWind' label.

Windy is web/Leaflet-only, online-only; ToS forbids caching/offline/redistribution and reconstructing model data, mandates a permanent clickable logo + attribution, bars direct-competition/no-added-value use, and the free tier is non-production. Ship Windy only as an optional online WebView tab under the paid Professional license (+ECMWF extra, internal-use-restricted) and only with written clearance from api@windy.com.

DO NOT statically link OpenCPN GPLv2-or-later source into a closed App Store App Store binary; GPL is non-transferable vs App Store terms (the VLC problem). Keep any GPL chart engine as an arm's-length contained component or rebuild on permissive GDAL/PROJ + custom S-52. Get IP counsel before committing.

MANDATORY user-facing disclaimer on all satellite/SDB layers: 'Supplemental aid, NOT for primary navigation — cross-reference official charts' (clouds hide reefs, imagery can paint out reefs, satellite-derived bathymetry only ~IHO ZOC-C / ~1.9m to ~10m depth).
Gate the entire on-demand chart-acquisition feature behind counsel review before launch — it is the single largest legal exposure in the product.
openQuestions: Which ENC path for Phase 2 — contain OpenCPN's GPL S-52 canvas (faster, but copyleft + OpenGL-on-iOS) or rebuild S-52 on GDAL/OGR (clean IP, more work)? Needs IP counsel + an effort estimate before committing.
Is the closed-source App Store binary acceptable to legal if any OpenCPN GPL code is embedded even arm's-length, or must we go 100% GDAL/permissive to be safe? Counsel decision gates Phase 2.
Will the on-demand tiler be hosted by us (server cost, source-credential custody, rate-limit/abuse exposure) or pushed to edge functions per-user? Affects unit economics and ToS posture.

What is the business model / pricing — does it fund Windy Professional (~EUR990/yr +ECMWF), Esri commercial license, and Navionics/C-MAP cartography IAP deals? These costs shape which Phase-3 sources are viable.
Does Windy grant written approval for a marine-nav app given the 'direct competition / significant added value' clause? If not, drop the Windy tab entirely.

Scope of 'all OpenCPN functionality' — do we carry the full 45+ plugin ecosystem (radar_pi, AISradar, oTCurrent, climatology) in v1, or only core nav + a plugin SDK in Phase 3? Radar especially is a large net-new effort.
For PredictWind GPX import, do exported routes reliably carry per-leg ETA/wind metadata, or only waypoints? Determines whether we can show departure-window context or just the geometry.

Target onboard-instrument matrix — which gateways/SignalK servers do we certify against (Yacht Devices, Digital Yacht, Actisense, Vesper, B&G), and do we need NMEA2000 PGN coverage beyond what SignalK normalizes?
Cloud sync backend for routes/marks — iCloud/CloudKit (Apple-only, free) vs own backend (cross-platform-future, cost)? Affects Android/web expansion later.

Do we want satellite-derived-bathymetry shading on Sentinel-2 (adds value in clear shallow water) in v1, or defer? It strengthens the differentiator but adds processing + a stronger accuracy disclaimer.

Top: native-style unified title/toolbar with traffic-light dots, app mark "Helm", an inline ⌘K command-search field, a GPS-fix chip, and the day/dusk/night pills. Below the toolbar a three-zone body: (1) a slim left vertical mode rail of icon buttons (chart, routes, AIS, tides, weather/GRIB, instruments, layers, settings) with the layers tool active; (2) a full-bleed center chart rendered as inline SVG (water + shallow polygons, tan land + coastline, dashed depth contours + soundings, range rings, compass rose, own-ship glyph with COG predictor, a magenta user route with diamond waypoints + brighter active leg, a distinct brighter PredictWind imported route polyline, AIS triangles with vectors incl. a red CPA-risk target, buoys, faint wind flow-ticks over the water, scale bar, lat/lon edge labels); (3) a right contextual "Layers & weather" stack panel with three grouped cards — Chart sources (Official ENC / Satellite / Navionics-with-lock badge), Windy weather layers (wind active, gust/rain/waves/current toggles), and a PredictWind routing card (imported optimal-route ETA + legend swatch). Bottom: full-width instrument strip of tabular-number tiles (SOG, COG, HDG, depth, TWS, TWA, position). A permanent Copernicus/supplemental attribution line sits in the chart's lower edge.

designs.newCapabilityUX: The right stack panel is where the additive, ToS-honest capabilities surface without displacing any OpenCPN function. Chart sources are stacked as composited layers: Official ENC (the trusted base, on), Satellite (Sentinel-2, on, carrying the permanent 'Copernicus Sentinel — supplemental aid, not for primary nav' attribution at the chart edge), and Navionics shown with a small lock/partnership badge and a muted/off state to telegraph that it is an online-only, partnership-gated source — never a scraped offline layer. Windy is presented as a weather-LAYER group inside the panel for discoverability, but the active 'wind' layer is rendered only as faint, honest flow-ticks over the water (the real on-chart weather is the owned GRIB renderer); the group header carries a small 'online' marker so it reads as the separate online Windy view, not an offline composite. The PredictWind card is explicitly an IMPORT: it shows an 'Imported optimal route' with its own brighter polyline and a swatch in the legend distinct from the user's magenta route, labelled as PredictWind-exported (truth-in-labelling) with its ETA — no live routing endpoint is implied. A second swatch in the same card points to 'Helm routing' as the owned alternative ('bring your PredictWind route — or let Helm route you'). On macOS the import affordance is native (file picker / drag-drop of .gpx/.grb), reinforced by the desktop-native toolbar.
designs.designRationale: This is the macOS sibling of the approved Helm concept, so it keeps the exact chart-first, calm-glass aesthetic, the same theme variables, the same SVG nautical vocabulary, and the same bottom instrument strip — then earns its 'native Mac' identity through a real window: traffic-light dots, a unified slim title/toolbar that hosts the command search and theme pills, and desktop-grade affordances (multi-source layer stack, file-import routing). The chart stays full-bleed and the chrome recedes (dark glass), exactly as on iPad, but the toolbar/menu density and the right 'Layers & weather' stack reflect a pointer-driven desktop where a navigator manages many composited sources at once. Critically, the redesign is ADDITIVE: ENC, routes/marks, AIS CPA/TCPA, tides, GRIB and instruments all remain; satellite, Windy, and PredictWind sit on top as clearly-labelled, legally-honest layers. Every legal guardrail is encoded into the pixels — Navionics wears a partnership lock and stays off, Windy is framed as a separate online layer rather than an offline composite, PredictWind is an import with a distinct route color and never a 'live overlay', and a permanent supplemental-navigation + Copernicus attribution line rides the chart edge.
designs.consistencyNotes: Stays native to macOS while matching its iPad/iPhone siblings: identical .helm theme tokens (night default + .dusk/.day), identical day/dusk/night pills, the same left mode rail, the same inline-SVG chart conventions (water/shallow polygons, tan land + coastline, dashed contours + soundings, magenta route + diamonds, own-ship + COG predictor, range rings, compass rose, AIS triangles with teal/amber/red CPA, buoys, scale bar, lat/lon edge labels), and the same tabular-number bottom instrument strip — so it is unmistakably the same product family. The platform-native deltas are deliberate and limited to what a Mac should do: a real window (traffic-light dots + unified toolbar) instead of a tablet status bar, a denser right 'Layers & weather' stack that suits a pointer + larger canvas, and file-picker/drag-drop import language for .gpx/.grb (vs the share-sheet handler on iOS). Two font weights only (400/500), sentence case with marine acronyms allowed, no font below 11px, Tabler outline icons throughout, all SVG fills/strokes driven by the --vars, and no position:fixed (a normal-flow faux window) so it renders safely in the 680px sandbox. The toggle reuses the same JS pattern as the other surfaces.

designs.newCapabilityUX: The interaction is: user taps the layers/download mode → drops into a 'select an area' state → drags a lasso rectangle over the coast (corner handles, live lat/lon + area readout) → the right panel becomes 'Charts for this area' and enumerates only the sources that legally cover that bbox. Honesty is built into the chrome, not buried in a EULA: every row wears a plain-language license chip. NOAA ENC reads 'Free • US public domain' and is pre-checked because it is the safe primary. Satellite imagery expands into a sub-picker where Sentinel-2 is the recommended, selectable option ('Open license • 10 m') and Bing/Google are visibly present but rendered inert with a 'personal use • check ToS — bring your own key' treatment so the product itself never server-fetches a prohibited tile; a persistent 'Supplemental aid — NOT for primary navigation, cross-reference official charts' line sits under any satellite/SDB selection. Navionics is shown but locked behind 'Requires Navionics account / partnership' rather than pretending an offline path exists. Selecting sources and a zoom range (Z12–Z17, with Z18 as an explicit opt-in chip) live-updates the estimated size (~340 MB). The offline-cache toggle and a 'Cache to Chart group' selector make clear the output is a standard mbtiles packed server-side and downloaded for offline MapLibre display + quilting, preserving OpenCPN's ChartLocker/Chart-group workflow. The primary action is literally 'Download mbtiles,' and an inline progress example (tiles fetched / total, percent) shows the running state. A footer carries the mandatory Copernicus attribution and a quiet 'chart acquisition is counsel-gated' note — turning the single biggest legal exposure into a transparent, in-UI contract with the user.
designs.designRationale: This surface had to do two contradictory things at once: feel like the SAME calm, chart-first Helm the user already approved, and introduce the product's most legally fraught new capability without a single dishonest pixel. So I kept the entire Helm shell intact — same dark glass chrome, same mode rail, same full-bleed SVG chart with all the nautical conventions, same bottom instrument strip — and let the new capability inhabit the existing right-panel slot rather than a foreign modal. The chart stays the hero; the acquisition sheet recedes into established chrome. The one genuinely new chart-canvas element, the dashed lasso, borrows the magenta route accent so it reads as 'a Helm selection,' consistent with the existing magenta waypoint language. Honesty-as-UI is the spine of the design: rather than hiding ToS in fine print, every source row wears a plain-language license chip, prohibited satellite sources (Bing/Google) are visibly present but inert and labelled 'bring your own key / check ToS,' Navionics is shown-but-locked, and Sentinel-2 is the recommended default precisely because it is the one redistributable commercial-safe source. The mandatory satellite disclaimer and Copernicus attribution are permanent, not dismissible. This keeps the surface additive to OpenCPN (mbtiles + Chart groups + quilting + offline cache are preserved), reflects the real server-side tiler architecture (the device downloads a packed mbtiles; credentials/source-swapping stay off-device), and makes the counsel-gated nature of the feature a visible, trustworthy contract instead of a liability.
designs.consistencyNotes: Stays native to iPadOS while matching its macOS/iPhone siblings: the shell, palette (.helm night/dusk/day vars), icon language (Tabler outline), tabular-number instruments, and magenta/teal/amber/red semantic colors are identical across all three targets because they're driven by the shared C++ nav-core's data and one brand token set — so a NOAA-ENC row, an AIS triangle, or a depth sounding looks the same everywhere. What's platform-native here: the lasso is a touch-first drag with finger-sized corner handles (not a trackpad marquee), the sheet is a right-docked panel sized for iPad portrait reach rather than a macOS inspector with menu-bar commands, and the share-sheet/file-handler hooks (.gpx/.grb/.mbtiles import) implied by 'bring your own' map to iPadOS document handlers. On macOS the same 'Charts for this area' surface would render as a proper window/inspector with menu items, file picker and drag-drop, and a marquee selection; on iPhone it would become a bottom sheet. The honesty chrome (license chips, disclaimers, attribution, counsel-gate footnote) is shared verbatim across platforms because it's a compliance requirement, not a platform style. Two font weights (400/500), sentence case, no sub-11px text, and no position:fixed are all honored so it renders cleanly in the 680px sandbox while still reading as the same product family.

designs.newCapabilityUX: The PredictWind feature is surfaced honestly as an IMPORT, never a live integration. The right panel header reads 'PredictWind optimal route' with a small italic 'imported - GPX from your Offshore app' provenance line and a distinct route badge, so the magenta line on the chart is clearly attributed to PredictWind (the only route allowed to carry that name). There is no 'compute PredictWind route' button anywhere. A separate 'Helm routing' affordance lives in the rail for the owned isochrone engine, keeping the truth-in-labelling boundary visible. The Windy layer appears only as a floating 'Windy wind - online' hint chip with an explicit online-only marker, never as a composited chart overlay or an offline-capable claim - tapping it would open a separate Windy WebView tab. Touch adaptations: the mode rail floats as a thumb-reachable glass pill with 44px targets, the right panel collapses to reclaim chart space, and instrument tiles enlarge for at-a-glance helm reading.
designs.designRationale: This is the same Helm product as the macOS sibling, re-skinned for touch rather than re-invented. It reuses the identical .helm theme tokens, the same nautical SVG vocabulary, and the same information architecture (top bar / left rail / full-bleed chart / right context panel / bottom instrument strip). The differences are strictly platform-native: the rail floats as a thumb-reachable glass pill with larger hit targets instead of docking to a window chrome; the right panel collapses with a single tap to maximise chart real estate on a tablet; instrument tiles are enlarged for glanceability at the helm. The chart depicts genuine OpenCPN-equivalent capability (depth contours, soundings, AIS with CPA/TCPA, range rings, compass rose) so 'keep all OpenCPN functionality' reads as preserved. New capabilities are additive and legally honest: the PredictWind route is an imported, distinctly-branded overlay (no impossible live integration, no credential replay), and Windy is a clearly-marked online-only hint, never an offline chart overlay. The calm dark glass chrome recedes so the chart leads.
designs.consistencyNotes: Stays native to iPadOS while matching siblings: same .helm tokens, same day/dusk/night top-bar toggle, same SVG nautical conventions and color semantics (teal/amber/red AIS, magenta route, brighter active leg) as macOS. Platform-native divergences are intentional and minimal - a floating thumb-reachable glass mode rail with 44px touch targets (vs the macOS docked rail), a one-tap collapsible right panel to reclaim chart space on a tablet, and enlarged instrument tiles for helm glanceability. Reflects the real architecture: this iPad UI is a SwiftUI+Metal clean re-implementation over the shared C++ nav core, not an OpenCPN port - so it carries the full feature set (ENC, AIS CPA/TCPA, GRIB, anchor watch) while being touch-first. PredictWind is shown only as an imported GPX overlay with explicit provenance (no live feed, no stored credentials), and Windy only as an online-only hint chip, matching the legal guardrails the macOS sibling also honours.

Everything is reachable one-handed in the thumb zone; the chart, route summary, and tab bar form a single vertical read order. No nested scroll — the sheet is a peek state, not a scroller.

Bottom tab bar: chart / routes / weather / AIS / more (Tabler outline icons, active = accent)
Theme toggle script (night/dusk/day on .helm root)
designs.newCapabilityUX: New/additive capabilities are surfaced without displacing OpenCPN parity. The layers FAB opens (conceptually) the chart-source stack where on-demand satellite (Sentinel-2) and imported BYO .mbtiles live alongside ENC/NOAA raster — so satellite is an additive layer, not a replacement, and any satellite layer carries the mandatory "Supplemental aid — not for primary navigation" disclaimer. Weather is its own tab: the chart overlay is always Helm's own offline GRIB (never Windy), with Windy reserved as a separate opt-in online tab. Routes tab is where "Import from PredictWind" (a user-exported .gpx, rendered as a distinctly-labelled imported overlay) sits next to "Helm Weather Routing" (the owned isochrone engine) — the underway view here only shows the ACTIVE route, truthfully labelled as Helm's. AIS retains full CPA/TCPA, and a red CPA-risk target is shown to demonstrate the collision logic survives the redesign. Anchor watch / MOB live under "more". On iPhone, NMEA/AIS/instrument data arrives over the network (SignalK/TCP/UDP/internal GPS) — reflected by the live GPS-fix chip rather than any wired-connection affordance.
designs.consistencyNotes: Same product family as the macOS/iPad siblings: identical .helm theme tokens (night default + dusk/day), the same chart-first "calm glass chrome recedes" philosophy, the same SVG nautical vocabulary (water/shallow polygons, dashed contours + soundings, magenta route w/ diamond waypoints + brighter active leg, own-ship COG predictor, teal/amber/red AIS, range rings, scale bar, lat/lon ticks), the same tabular-number instrument tiles, and the same day/dusk/night toggle as a first-class control. It stays native to iOS by collapsing the desktop's left mode-rail + right context panel + bottom strip into the platform-idiomatic iPhone trio — top safe-area status row, a single peeking bottom sheet, and a bottom tab bar — sized for one-handed underway use, with Dynamic Island + safe-area framing, a thumb-reachable FAB, and touch-first hit targets. Functionality is preserved, not removed: routes/AIS-CPA/TCPA/tides/weather-GRIB/anchor-watch all remain reachable via the tab bar and "more", just re-homed for a 390pt portrait canvas. Two weights (400/500), sentence case, Tabler outline icons, no sub-11px type — matching the sibling rules exactly.
designs.designRationale: Underway on a phone, the helmsman glances for one second at a time, one-handed, often in spray and glare. So the design is ruthlessly glanceable: the chart is the whole screen, chrome is translucent dark glass that recedes, and only the three numbers that matter while steering (SOG, COG, depth) sit fixed at the top with a live GPS-fix chip so trust in the position is always visible. The single most important ongoing task — following the active route — is reduced to one always-visible line in a peeking bottom sheet ("to WP3 • 3.2 NM • ETA 14:38") with a tiny bearing rosette, so the next decision is answerable without opening anything. A red CPA-risk AIS target is left on the chart to prove the collision-avoidance brain is intact in the compact UI. The day/dusk/night toggle stays first-class because it is a real safety control at the helm (night-vision preservation), not a cosmetic theme. Heavier tasks (route editing, weather routing, layer/source management, anchor watch, MOB) are deliberately pushed one tap away into the tab bar and sheet, keeping the underway surface calm while preserving full OpenCPN-class capability additively.

issues.target: chartEngine / onDemandChartPipeline — Esri 'World Imagery (for Export)' framing
issues.problem: The artifact lists Esri as 'gray' and shippable under a paid Master License with a '100k-tile cap', and Phase 3 proposes shipping it as a licensed offline imagery layer. This is directionally correct but risks being read as 'we can offer global Esri imagery offline to end users.' The Esri 'for Export' path is constrained to use INSIDE Esri's own SDK/ArcGIS app ecosystem, the underlying imagery is third-party (Maxar et al.) with its own downstream-license/audit obligations, and the 100k-tile cap is per-export — not a product-wide allowance. A native non-ArcGIS app caching and redistributing Esri tiles to thousands of users is not what that license grants.
issues.correction: State explicitly: Esri World Imagery offline is usable ONLY under a signed Esri commercial agreement, within Esri's own SDK/runtime, subject to the per-export tile cap and downstream-customer license + audit terms; it is NOT a freely-redistributable offline source for a third-party native binary. Treat as a Phase-3 'pursue a paid Esri deal or drop it' item, not a planned feature.

issues.target: weatherLayerApproach / windyIntegration — 'EUR990+yr paid' Windy pricing and competition clause
issues.problem: The artifact correctly bars Windy as a chart overlay, bars caching/offline, and gates it behind the paid Professional tier and written clearance. One residual overclaim risk: it still treats an optional online Windy WebView tab as a plannable Phase-3 deliverable. The research is explicit that a marine-nav app is plausibly 'direct competition' / lacks 'significant added value' under Windy's ToS, and that the paid implementation 'cannot be solely in the paid part' of the app. So even the gated tab may be DENIED, not merely expensive.
issues.correction: Label the Windy tab as conditional-on-approval, not a committed deliverable: 'Windy online tab ships ONLY IF api@windy.com grants written clearance that a marine-nav app is permitted under the direct-competition/added-value clause; absent that clearance, the feature is dropped entirely and own-GRIB is the only weather.' This already appears as an open question — promote it to a hard gate in the Phase-3 scope line, not just the open-questions list.

issues.target: predictwindIntegration / phasedPlan Phase 2 — PredictWind GRIB1 import labelling
issues.problem: Track A allows importing 'PredictWind GRIB1 files they export' and rendering them. Accurate that GRIB export exists and is user-initiated. The subtle risk: PredictWind GRIB is derived from licensed model blends (AROME/ECMWF/UKMO) that PredictWind itself pays to redistribute under restriction. Importing a user's own exported file for their own display is fine; any future move to cache/redistribute/share those GRIBs cross-device via the cloud-sync feature (offlineSync mentions optional cloud sync for routes) could implicate the underlying model licenses (notably ECMWF 'internal use only' restrictions).
issues.correction: Add a guardrail: PredictWind/ECMWF-derived GRIB and any imported PredictWind route stay device-local and user-private; do NOT route them through the optional CloudKit/own-backend sync as shareable objects, and do not server-store them. Keep GRIB import strictly client-side display of the user's own export, consistent with the single-person-license constraint already stated for credentials.

overclaims: Esri World Imagery as a near-term licensed offline layer (Phase 3) understates that it is SDK-locked, per-export-capped, and carries third-party (Maxar) downstream-license + audit obligations — not a clean redistributable source.

The optional online Windy WebView tab is still framed as a plannable Phase-3 deliverable; under Windy's direct-competition/added-value clause it may be flatly denied, so it is approval-contingent, not merely cost-contingent.

Imported PredictWind GRIB is implicitly safe to handle like any GRIB; its ECMWF/AROME/UKMO-derived content carries redistribution restrictions, so it must be kept device-local and excluded from any cloud-sync/sharing path.
verdicts.verdict: SOUND — APPROVE WITH MINOR CORRECTIONS. I tried to refute the five highest-risk overclaim categories and the artifact holds on all of them. Specifically: (1) Google/Bing/Navionics tiles are correctly placed in 'NOT shippable / BYO-only' with the exact ToS rationale (no scrape/cache/redistribute; Bing EOL 2028; Navionics SDK is online-only) — no claim of free caching/redistribution survives anywhere in the artifact, and the on-demand feature is gated behind counsel review as 'the single largest legal exposure.' (2) PredictWind is correctly stated to have NO public API ('confirmed verbatim'), routing is declared IMPOSSIBLE to compute/overlay live, and the only paths are user-initiated GPX/GRIB import with a hard truth-in-labelling rule and a no-credential-replay constraint — no phantom routing endpoint is architected. (3) Windy is correctly described as owning its own Leaflet map (cannot composite on our chart), online-only, uncacheable, logo-mandatory, free-tier-non-production, paid ~EUR990/yr, and possibly barred as direct competition — it is never marketed as offline or as a chart overlay. (4) The 'OpenCPN runs on iPhone' framing is explicitly REFUTED: iOS targets are declared a clean re-implementation (not a port), citing GPL-vs-App-Store (the VLC problem), wxWidgets-desktop-UI, sandbox-forbids-serial/USB, and touch-vs-mouse, with qtVlm/Aqua Map as precedent. (5) iOS serial/USB NMEA is correctly declared NON-existent and rebuilt as SignalK/TCP/UDP/BLE/internal-GPS, autopilot output over network, serial only on macOS. Offline storage realism is sober (GB-scale satellite, Z16/17 cap, Sentinel-2 at 10m flagged, mandatory 'supplemental — not for primary nav' disclaimer). The GPL boundary is handled correctly (arm's-length contained component OR GDAL/permissive rebuild, gated on IP counsel; no static-link of GPL into a closed App Store binary). The three issues found are all MINOR refinements at the edges (Esri scope, Windy approval-gating, PredictWind-GRIB cloud-sync hygiene) — none rise to blocker or major, and none contradict the core legal guardrails. This document is unusually honest and ships as a defensible spec once the three minor clarifications are folded into the guardrail register.

issues.problem: The fragment is truncated mid-CSS — it ends inside the .panel rule at `border-left:1px` with no closing brace, no chart SVG, no instrument strip, and no theme <script>. As provided it will not render a complete window; the unterminated CSS rule can also swallow following markup.
issues.correction: Supply the complete fragment: close the .panel rule and all remaining CSS, include the full-bleed inline SVG chart, the right layers/weather panel, the bottom instrument strip, and the end <script> theme toggle. Re-verify in the 680px sandbox before sign-off.

issues.problem: Two defects. (1) The whole fragment is wrapped in `<![CDATA[ ... ]]>`; an HTML fragment is not XML, so the literal `<![CDATA[` text will render as visible page content. (2) The fragment is truncated mid-SVG `<path>` so the chart and the rest of the layout are missing.
issues.correction: Remove the CDATA wrapper entirely (start directly with the sr-only <h2>). Complete the SVG and the remaining markup (touch rail, route panel, instrument strip, script), then verify in-sandbox.

issues.problem: Uses host design-system tokens with no fallback: `background:var(--color-background-secondary)` on the device surface, `border-radius:var(--border-radius-xl)`, and `font-family:var(--font-sans)`. Inside a self-contained, .helm-scoped branded product these vars are undefined, so the device surface loses its background (frame floats on transparent), corners go square, and the body font falls back to the browser default. Surface 1 hardcodes --color-background-secondary:#05080c locally and surface 4 gives a fallback; surface 3 does neither — an inconsistency that also breaks the 'device frames sit on a surface' rule.
issues.correction: Provide fallbacks exactly like the other surfaces, e.g. var(--color-background-secondary,#05080c), and replace var(--font-sans)/var(--border-radius-xl) with the literal stack used elsewhere (-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,...) and a fixed radius. Keep the secondary surface opaque so the iPad frame reads as sitting on it.

issues.problem: Corrupted color token: `--inkdim:#b89madd;` is not a valid hex (7 chars, contains the letter m). It is duplicated on the next line by a valid `--inkdim:#b39a78;` so the cascade currently masks it, but it is a broken declaration that signals copy-paste rot and will surface if the duplicate is ever removed.

The dusk scheme diverges across surfaces and from the spec. Spec dusk = warm ambers/browns (--bg:#161019 --water:#2a1d24 ...). Surface 1 matches the spec exactly; surface 4 uses a different, much warmer/browner ramp (--bg:#1a1208 --water:#241808 --chrome:#241606). Surfaces 2 and 3 (as far as visible) do not even define a .dusk override at all, so pressing Dusk there changes nothing. This is a cross-surface palette inconsistency — the same product renders three different dusk modes.
issues.correction: Adopt one canonical dusk ramp (the spec's) in a shared block reused verbatim across all four surfaces, and ensure every surface that exposes a Dusk pill actually defines the .dusk overrides.

issues.problem: Sounding numbers use font-size="11" in SVG user units. Because the chart uses preserveAspectRatio slice and scales UP to the rendered width, effective pixel size is at or above the 11px floor in these viewBoxes — acceptable — but it is right on the boundary and relies on the SVG never being rendered smaller than its viewBox width. No margin for the macOS window where the chart column is narrower.
issues.correction: Bump SVG label font-size to 12 (or compute against the smallest rendered width) so soundings/labels never dip below 11px effective in any surface, especially the narrower desktop chart column.

issues.target: Cross-surface — GPS-fix chip & search placeholder copy
issues.problem: Minor iconography/copy drift, not a rule break: the fix chip uses ti-gps (surface 2), ti-satellite (surface 3), and a bare colored dot (surface 1/4); search placeholder text differs ('Search charts, routes, marks' vs 'Search charts, places, MMSI'). Acronyms (RTK/GPS/AIS/MMSI/SOG/COG) are correctly left uppercase per the rule.
issues.correction: Standardize on one fix-chip icon and one search placeholder string across all four surfaces for a single coherent product.

overclaims: Surfaces 1 and 3 are truncated in the supplied material, so the review of their lower regions (instrument strip, AIS card, script, scale bar) is necessarily partial — a clean full copy must be re-checked before final sign-off.
Effective on-screen font sizes for SVG text and the rounding of displayed numbers were reasoned about from the source, not measured in a live 680px render; confirm with a screenshot.
verdicts.verdict: CHANGES REQUIRED. No position:fixed, no emoji/hand-drawn icons, no font-weight 600/700, no ALL-CAPS non-acronyms, and sr-only h2 summaries are present on all four — the core render rules are respected and the night palette is consistent and dark-safe. But two surfaces are unshippable as given: the macOS window (1) is truncated mid-CSS and the iPadOS-landscape (3) is both wrapped in a literal <![CDATA[ that will print on-page and truncated mid-SVG. Additional fixes: surface 3's undefined host vars (--color-background-secondary/--font-sans/--border-radius-xl) with no fallback break the 'frame on a surface' rule; the iPhone dusk block has a malformed --inkdim:#b89madd hex; and the dusk scheme is inconsistent across the four surfaces (two don't define it). Fix the blockers, unify the dusk ramp, repair the broken token and host-var fallbacks, then re-verify in the sandbox.

brief.productConcept: Helm is a modern, native-on-Apple chartplotter that carries the full OpenCPN feature set forward additively across macOS, iPad, and iPhone. It keeps every OpenCPN capability (ENC display, GRIB weather, isochrone routing, AIS CPA/TCPA, tides/currents, anchor watch, GPX/marks) but delivers it through touch-first SwiftUI/Metal UIs on iOS/iPadOS and a native SwiftUI/AppKit app on macOS, all sitting on one shared C++ nav core. Its three differentiators over OpenCPN: (1) on-demand "lasso an area, fetch charts, cache offline" chart acquisition — the live version of ChartLocker's idea, built only on clean sources; (2) an owned, offline isochrone weather-routing engine plus honest "import your PredictWind route" interop; (3) own-GRIB weather layers composited natively on the chart, with optional online Windy as a clearance-gated extra. Positioning: "Bring your PredictWind route — or let Helm route you," on charts you assembled yourself, offline-first.
feasibilityTruths: OpenCPN-on-iOS is a REBUILD, not a port. wxWidgets is a desktop mouse-driven toolkit, GPLv2-or-later conflicts with App Store non-transferable terms (the VLC problem), and the iOS sandbox forbids serial/USB. iPad/iPhone are clean re-implementations on a shared C++ core; only macOS can reuse OpenCPN's actual canvas. Precedent that native iOS chartplotters work: qtVlm and Aqua Map.

ChartLocker's on-demand reality: ChartLocker today ships pre-built regional mbtiles ZIPs. The live 'select-an-area, fetch-on-demand' version IS buildable — but only on clean sources (Sentinel-2, NOAA ENC/NCDS). The specific Navionics-raster + Google/Bing tile scraping ChartLocker is associated with violates those providers' ToS and cannot ship in our product; we preserve that workflow via 'import your own .mbtiles' (BYO).

PredictWind has NO public API — confirmed verbatim in their FAQ, and not on their roadmap. Their optimal routing runs server-side in the PredictWind cloud and is delivered only into their own Offshore app and OEM-embedded MFD copies (Raymarine/B&G run PredictWind's own app, not a licensable feed). A live 'compute/overlay the PredictWind route' integration is IMPOSSIBLE and must not be promised. Only path: the user exports a GPX route / GRIB from their own logged-in app and imports it, client-side, labelled honestly as imported.

Windy CANNOT be a chart overlay. The Windy Map Forecast API is a Leaflet library that owns its own map instance (staff-confirmed it cannot be added to another map), forbids caching/offline/redistribution, mandates a permanent logo, is GFS-only/non-production on the free tier, and may bar marine-nav apps as 'direct competition.' It can only ever be an optional online WebView tab — and only if Windy grants written clearance. The product's real, ownable, offline weather is our own GRIB renderer (same model as OpenCPN's GRIB plugin).

MapLibre fundamentally cannot render S-57/S-52 — it uses the Mapbox Style Spec and has no concept of IHO symbology, safety contours, or Day/Dusk/Night palettes. True ENC needs a dedicated S-52 engine. A hybrid two-renderer design is load-bearing, not a nicety: MapLibre Native (Metal) for raster/satellite/weather/quilted-mbtiles, a contained S-52/S-57 engine composited on top for genuine vector charts. NOAA NCDS raster mbtiles let us defer live S-52 rendering to Phase 2.

Satellite imagery is a supplemental aid, NOT primary navigation. Clouds hide reefs, imagery can paint reefs out, Sentinel-2 is 10m resolution, and satellite-derived bathymetry is only ~IHO ZOC-C accuracy. A permanent 'supplemental — cross-reference official charts' disclaimer is mandatory on all satellite/SDB layers.

iOS has no serial/USB NMEA — boat connectivity is net-new, ingested over the network: SignalK (WebSocket) as primary, NMEA0183 over TCP/UDP (port 10110) as fallback, BLE for handheld GPS, internal GPS. Autopilot output goes over the network on iOS. Serial/USB is allowed only on macOS. This pattern is proven by Aqua Map, NMEAremote, qtVlm.
brief.architectureSummary: One shared C++ "nav core" compiled into all three Apple targets, with platform-native front-ends — NOT a port and NOT a cross-platform UI toolkit. The core holds everything platform-agnostic: chart access + quilting + Chart Groups; the S-57/S-52/CM93 ENC engine (a contained GPL component OR a GDAL/OGR + custom-S-52 rebuild, behind a stable interface to firewall copyleft); raster/mbtiles/PMTiles via MapLibre Native (Metal); GRIB1/2 parsing + weather rendering; the isochrone router + polar handling; AIS with CPA/TCPA/guard-zones/SART/MOB; tides/currents harmonics; anchor-watch logic; the SignalK/NMEA0183 connection manager + normalization; and GPX/ENC/mbtiles/polar/GRIB I/O. Front-ends: SwiftUI + Metal (touch) on iOS/iPadOS, SwiftUI/AppKit (menus, multi-window, trackpad, drag-drop, .gpx/.grb/.mbtiles file handlers) on macOS. Rendering is a hybrid two-renderer design: MapLibre Native for raster/satellite/weather/quilted-mbtiles, plus a dedicated S-52 ENC engine composited on top for true vector charts. The on-demand TILER runs server/edge-side (not on-device) so source credentials and source-swapping stay off the device. Rejected UI alternatives: React Native (JS-bridge overhead wrong for a 60fps chart canvas), Flutter (non-native widgets undercut the macOS-native goal and still need C++ FFI), Qt (C++-native but desktop-first with non-native iOS ergonomics — acceptable only as a fallback). GPL boundary requires IP counsel sign-off before any OpenCPN source is embedded; never static-link GPLv2+ into a closed App Store binary.
brief.chartAcquisition: Pipeline: user lassos a bbox + picks max zoom (cap Z16/17, opt-in Z18) -> server/edge tiler builds an XYZ tile list -> fetches source -> georeferences (gdalwarp+GCPs only for ad-hoc imagery; Sentinel-2/NOAA already georeferenced) -> packs mbtiles via GDAL/gdal2tiles with the TMS Y-flip -> device downloads packed mbtiles once, caches offline permanently, displays via MapLibre + quilting + Chart Groups. CLEAN (server-fetch+host+cache OK): Sentinel-2/Copernicus (free, commercial-OK, attribution 'Copernicus Sentinel data [Year]', 10m), NOAA ENC + NCDS raster mbtiles (US public domain), OpenSeaMap (ODbL, seamarks overlay only). GRAY (paid-deal-only): Esri 'World Imagery (for Export)' is usable ONLY under a signed Esri commercial agreement, inside Esri's own SDK, subject to a per-export ~100k-tile cap and downstream Maxar license + audit terms — NOT a freely-redistributable third-party source; Phase-3 pursue-or-drop, not a default. BLOCKED (BYO-only): Google + Bing (ToS bars cache/bulk-download; Bing EOL 2028), Navionics (official API is online-display-only; scraping prohibited). The full ChartLocker workflow is preserved via 'import my own .mbtiles' so the company never touches a prohibited tile. Whole feature gated behind counsel review — the single largest legal exposure.
brief.weatherStack: PRIMARY and ownable: own GRIB layers composited natively over the chart (GFS/GFS-Wave/RTOFS by default — free, offline, unbranded, ToS-free, same model as OpenCPN's GRIB plugin), with forecast age/staleness shown prominently. This is the product's real weather. SECONDARY and approval-contingent: Windy ONLY as an optional online-only WebView tab, NEVER a chart overlay (its Leaflet API owns its own map and cannot composite on ours), carrying a mandatory permanent logo + attribution, no caching/offline, GFS-only/non-production free tier, ~EUR990/yr Professional + ECMWF extra. The Windy tab ships ONLY IF api@windy.com gives written clearance that a marine-nav app is permitted under the direct-competition/added-value clause — otherwise dropped entirely. PredictWind-exported GRIB may be imported for the user's own offline display but is ECMWF/AROME/UKMO-derived, so it stays device-local and is excluded from any cloud-sync/sharing path.
legalGuardrails: Google Maps tiles: DO NOT scrape, bulk-download, cache, or ship offline — ToS explicitly bars pre-fetch/store/cache. User-BYO only; never server-fetch or host.
Bing Maps imagery: DO-NOT-BUILD — no offline support and Bing Maps Enterprise is EOL June 30 2028 with no new licensees since June 2024.

Esri/ArcGIS World Imagery offline is usable ONLY under a signed Esri commercial agreement, INSIDE Esri's own SDK/runtime, subject to a per-export ~100k-tile cap and downstream third-party (Maxar) license + audit obligations. It is NOT freely redistributable to thousands of users from a third-party native binary. Phase-3 'pursue a paid Esri deal or drop it' — not a planned feature.

Navionics: DO NOT scrape/rasterize web tiles (the ChartLocker method) — ToS forbids non-official-API access and reverse engineering. The only legitimate path is the approval-gated paid Garmin/Navionics Web API or Mobile SDK, which is ONLINE-display-only; offline on-demand Navionics is not offered even on the paid path.

Sentinel-2/Copernicus: SAFE for commercial use, redistribution, derivatives — but ONLY with permanent attribution 'Copernicus Sentinel data [Year]' / 'Contains modified Copernicus Sentinel data [Year]'. Disclose its 10m resolution limit.
NOAA ENC/RNC are US public domain (bulk-OK; prefer ENC/NCDS, RNC seamless services retired 2021/2022). OpenSeaMap is ODbL (attribution + share-alike) — seamarks overlay only, never a primary chart.

PredictWind has NO public API and won't build one — do NOT architect against a routing endpoint, do NOT promise an in-app PredictWind route overlay, and NEVER store/replay user PredictWind credentials server-side (single-person license; 5 devices/IPs in 6h triggers forced logout + password reset). Only a route/GRIB the user themselves exported may carry the 'PredictWind' label.

Imported PredictWind GRIB (and any imported PredictWind route) carries ECMWF/AROME/UKMO redistribution restrictions (notably ECMWF 'internal use only'). Keep it strictly device-local and user-private: do NOT route it through CloudKit/own-backend sync as a shareable object, and do not server-store it. Client-side display of the user's own export only.

Windy: online-only, web/Leaflet-only, cannot composite on our chart; ToS forbids caching/offline/redistribution/model reconstruction, mandates a permanent clickable logo + attribution, free tier is non-production. Ship ONLY as an optional online WebView tab under the paid Professional license AND with WRITTEN clearance from api@windy.com that a marine-nav app is permitted under the direct-competition/added-value clause. No clearance = drop the feature entirely.

Do NOT statically link OpenCPN GPLv2-or-later source into a closed App Store binary (GPL is non-transferable vs App Store terms — the VLC problem). Keep any GPL chart engine as an arm's-length contained component or rebuild on permissive GDAL/PROJ + custom S-52. IP counsel must sign off before any embedding.

MANDATORY permanent user-facing disclaimer on all satellite / satellite-derived-bathymetry layers: 'Supplemental aid, NOT for primary navigation — cross-reference official charts' (clouds and imagery can hide/paint-out reefs; SDB only ~IHO ZOC-C / ~1.9m–10m).
Gate the entire on-demand chart-acquisition feature behind counsel review before launch — it is the single largest legal exposure in the product.

phasedPlan.detail: IP counsel on the OpenCPN GPL boundary (contained component vs GDAL rebuild) before any code embeds GPL; confirm Copernicus/NOAA/OpenSeaMap attribution; document PredictWind has no API and open written terms check with api@windy.com; stand up the shared C++ core building green on macOS/iPadOS/iOS with MapLibre Native (Metal) rendering a basic mbtiles in an empty SwiftUI shell on each platform; create the attribution/ToS guardrail register.

phasedPlan.phase: Phase 1 — MVP: native chart + safe on-demand charts + connectivity + own GRIB
phasedPlan.detail: SwiftUI/MapLibre shell on all three; NOAA NCDS raster + Sentinel-2 select-area->server-tiler(TMS Y-flip)->mbtiles->offline pipeline; BYO 'import my own .mbtiles' (the ChartLocker bridge); SignalK + NMEA0183-over-TCP/UDP + internal GPS; AIS with CPA/TCPA + alarms; routes/marks/tracks via GPX; own-GRIB weather (GFS/GFS-Wave/RTOFS) composited and offline-cached; mandatory 'supplemental, not for primary nav' disclaimer on satellite.

phasedPlan.phase: Phase 2 — True ENC + owned routing + anchor watch + PredictWind import
phasedPlan.detail: Contained S-52/S-57/CM93 ENC engine (GPL component or GDAL+custom S-52) with Day/Dusk/Night palettes, quilting, Chart Groups; the owned isochrone 'Helm Weather Routing' engine + polar library/editor (.pol/.csv) on free NOAA GRIB; anchor watch via background 'Always' location + region monitoring + drag notifications; tides/currents harmonics + dashboard instruments; PredictWind Track A — client-side GPX/GRIB1 import via file picker and iOS .gpx/.grb share-sheet handlers, kept device-local and out of cloud sync.

phasedPlan.phase: Phase 3 — Licensed premium charts + (conditional) Windy + plugin extensibility
phasedPlan.detail: Formal cartography deals for Navionics/C-MAP/NV-Charts via official SDK/IAP (iNavX/X-Traverse model), online or licensed-offline, never scraped; PURSUE-OR-DROP a paid Esri 'World Imagery (for Export)' deal inside Esri's SDK within the cap; ship the online Windy WebView tab ONLY IF api@windy.com grants written clearance under the direct-competition clause, else drop it; plugin/extensibility SDK to carry forward the OpenCPN ecosystem (radar, climatology, oTCurrent); autopilot output over network; counsel sign-off on every source before commercial launch.

risks: ENC-path decision unresolved: contain OpenCPN's GPL S-52 canvas (faster, but copyleft + OpenGL-on-iOS) vs rebuild S-52 on GDAL/OGR (clean IP, more work). Needs IP counsel + effort estimate; gates Phase 2.
Closed-source App Store binary may be incompatible with ANY embedded OpenCPN GPL code even arm's-length — counsel may force a 100% GDAL/permissive path, expanding Phase-2 scope.
On-demand tiler hosting model (self-hosted server cost + source-credential custody + rate-limit/abuse exposure vs per-user edge functions) drives unit economics and ToS posture — undecided.
Windy marine-nav tab may be DENIED outright under the direct-competition clause regardless of willingness to pay; plan must survive with own-GRIB as the only weather.
Business model/pricing must fund Windy Professional (~EUR990/yr +ECMWF), any Esri commercial license, and Navionics/C-MAP IAP deals — these costs determine which Phase-3 sources are even viable.

Scope of 'all OpenCPN functionality' — the 45+ plugin ecosystem (radar_pi especially) is a large net-new effort; risk of over-promising parity if we ship core + a plugin SDK rather than every plugin in v1.
PredictWind GPX exports may carry only waypoints, losing per-leg ETA/wind metadata — limits how much departure-window context we can show from an imported route.

Anchor-watch background 'Always' location is the most likely App Store review friction point; naive continuous polling gets suspended, so it needs region/significant-location monitoring done robustly.

Satellite/SDB accuracy liability — even with the mandatory disclaimer, painting imagery as a nav layer carries reputational and possibly legal risk if a user grounds; disclaimer prominence and SDB shading scope must be conservative.
Onboard-instrument certification matrix (which gateways/SignalK servers, NMEA2000 PGN coverage beyond SignalK normalization) is open and affects perceived reliability at launch.
